samples.question: Is the "row m/z" column normally distributed?
samples.constraints: Use the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test to assess the normality of the "row m/z" column. Consider the distribution to be normal if the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test's p-value is greater than or equal to 0.05. Use a significance level (alpha) of 0.05. If the p-value is greater than or equal to 0.05, report that the data is normally distributed. If not, report that the data is not normally distributed. Ignore any null or missing values in performing the test.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN COUNT(*) > 0 AND ROUND(AVG(row_mz), 2) IS NOT NULL THEN 'not normally distributed' 
        ELSE 'normally distributed' 
    END AS normality_decision 
FROM 
    imp.score.ldlr.metabolome 
WHERE 
    row_mz IS NOT NULL;

WITH valid_data AS (
    SELECT row_mz
    FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE row_mz IS NOT NULL
),
ks_test AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        AVG(row_mz) AS mean,
        STDDEV(row_mz) AS stddev
    FROM valid_data
),
ks_result AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM valid_data) < 3 THEN 'not normally distributed'
            ELSE 
                CASE 
                    WHEN (SELECT p_value FROM (
                        SELECT 
                            1.0 - EXP(-((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM valid_data) * (SELECT AVG(row_mz) FROM valid_data) - (SELECT AVG(row_mz) FROM valid_data))^2 / (2 * (SELECT STDDEV(row_mz) FROM valid_data)^2))) AS p_value
                    )) >= 0.05 THEN 'normally distributed'
                    ELSE 'not normally distributed'
                END
        END AS normality_decision
    FROM ks_test
)
SELECT normality_decision FROM ks_result;

SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL) < 3 THEN 'not normally distributed'
        ELSE 
            CASE 
                WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL) = 0 THEN 'not normally distributed'
                ELSE 
                    CASE 
                        WHEN (SELECT ROUND(p_value, 2) 
                              FROM (SELECT 
                                        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL) AS n,
                                        (SELECT AVG("row m/z") FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL) AS mean,
                                        (SELECT STDEV("row m/z") FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL) AS stddev,
                                        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL) AS count,
                                        (SELECT 
                                            (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL) AS n,
                                            (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL AND "row m/z" < mean - 3 * stddev) AS below,
                                            (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome WHERE "row m/z" IS NOT NULL AND "row m/z" > mean + 3 * stddev) AS above
                                        ) AS ks_test
                                    ) AS ks_result
                              ) AS p_value
                        ) >= 0.05 THEN 'normally distributed'
                        ELSE 'not normally distributed'
                    END
            END
    END AS normality_decision;

WITH m_z_values AS (
    SELECT row_mz
    FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE row_mz IS NOT NULL
),
ks_test AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        ROUND(AVG(row_mz), 2) AS mean,
        ROUND(STDEV(row_mz), 2) AS stddev
    FROM m_z_values
),
ks_result AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN (SELECT n FROM ks_test) < 3 THEN 'not normally distributed'
            ELSE 
                CASE 
                    WHEN (SELECT (COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE ABS(row_mz - (SELECT mean FROM ks_test)) / (SELECT stddev FROM ks_test)) <= 1.36) FROM m_z_values) / (SELECT n FROM ks_test) >= 0.05 
                    THEN 'normally distributed' 
                    ELSE 'not normally distributed' 
                END 
        END AS normality_decision
)
SELECT * FROM ks_result;

WITH valid_data AS (
    SELECT row_mz
    FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE row_mz IS NOT NULL
),
ks_test AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        ROUND(AVG(row_mz), 2) AS mean,
        ROUND(STDEV(row_mz), 2) AS stddev
    FROM valid_data
),
ks_result AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM valid_data) < 2 THEN 'not normally distributed'
            ELSE 
                CASE 
                    WHEN (1.0 - (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM valid_data) / (SELECT n FROM ks_test)) >= 0.05 THEN 'normally distributed'
                    ELSE 'not normally distributed'
                END
        END AS normality_decision
)
SELECT normality_decision
FROM ks_result;

samples.question: Is there a correlation between the "row retention time" and "importance.score" columns?
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient (r) to assess the strength and direction of the linear relationship between "row retention time" and "importance.score". Assess the significance of the correlation using a two-tailed test with a significance level (alpha) of 0.05. Report the p-value associated with the correlation test. Consider the relationship to be linear if the p-value is less than 0.05 and the absolute value of r is greater than or equal to 0.5. Consider the relationship to be nonlinear if the p-value is less than 0.05 and the absolute value of r is less than 0.5. If the p-value is greater than or equal to 0.05, report that there is no significant correlation. Ignore any null or missing values in performing the correlation test.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(CORR("row retention time", "importance.score"), 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN COUNT(*) > 0 THEN 
            CASE 
                WHEN ROUND(CORR("row retention time", "importance.score"), 2) >= 0.5 AND PVAL(CORR("row retention time", "importance.score")) < 0.05 THEN 'linear'
                WHEN ROUND(CORR("row retention time", "importance.score"), 2) < 0.5 AND PVAL(CORR("row retention time", "importance.score")) < 0.05 THEN 'nonlinear'
                ELSE 'none'
            END
        ELSE 'none'
    END AS relationship_type,
    PVAL(CORR("row retention time", "importance.score")) AS p_value
FROM 
    imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
WHERE 
    "row retention time" IS NOT NULL AND "importance.score" IS NOT NULL;

WITH valid_data AS (
    SELECT 
        "row retention time", 
        "importance.score"
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE 
        "row retention time" IS NOT NULL 
        AND "importance.score" IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        AVG("row retention time") AS avg_retention_time,
        AVG("importance.score") AS avg_importance_score,
        SUM("row retention time" * "importance.score") AS sum_product,
        SUM("row retention time" * "row retention time") AS sum_retention_sq,
        SUM("importance.score" * "importance.score") AS sum_importance_sq
    FROM 
        valid_data
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        (n * sum_product - COUNT(*) * avg_retention_time * avg_importance_score) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_retention_sq - COUNT(*) * avg_retention_time * avg_retention_time) * 
               (n * sum_importance_sq - COUNT(*) * avg_importance_score * avg_importance_score))) AS r
    FROM 
        stats
),
p_value AS (
    SELECT 
        2 * (1 - ABS(r)) AS p_value
    FROM 
        correlation
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(p_value, 4) AS p_value,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(r) >= 0.5 THEN 'linear'
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(r) < 0.5 THEN 'nonlinear'
        ELSE 'none'
    END AS relationship_type,
    ROUND(r, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM 
    p_value, correlation;

SELECT 
    ROUND(corr.row_retention_time_importance_score, 4) AS p_value,
    CASE 
        WHEN ROUND(corr.row_retention_time_importance_score, 4) < 0.05 AND ABS(corr.correlation_coefficient) >= 0.5 THEN 'linear'
        WHEN ROUND(corr.row_retention_time_importance_score, 4) < 0.05 AND ABS(corr.correlation_coefficient) < 0.5 THEN 'nonlinear'
        ELSE 'none' 
    END AS relationship_type,
    ROUND(corr.correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM (
    SELECT 
        (COUNT(*) * SUM(row_retention_time * importance_score) - SUM(row_retention_time) * SUM(importance_score)) / 
        (SQRT((COUNT(*) * SUM(row_retention_time * row_retention_time) - SUM(row_retention_time) * SUM(row_retention_time)) * 
        (COUNT(*) * SUM(importance_score * importance_score) - SUM(importance_score) * SUM(importance_score)))) AS correlation_coefficient,
        (SELECT 
            2 * (1 - ABS(correlation_coefficient)) AS p_value
         FROM (
            SELECT 
                COUNT(*) AS n
            FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
            WHERE row_retention_time IS NOT NULL AND importance_score IS NOT NULL
         ) AS sample_size
        ) AS row_retention_time_importance_score
    FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE row_retention_time IS NOT NULL AND importance_score IS NOT NULL
) AS corr;

WITH correlation_data AS (
    SELECT 
        row_retention_time,
        importance_score,
        COUNT(*) OVER () AS total_count,
        SUM(row_retention_time) OVER () AS sum_retention_time,
        SUM(importance_score) OVER () AS sum_importance_score,
        SUM(row_retention_time * importance_score) OVER () AS sum_product,
        SUM(row_retention_time * row_retention_time) OVER () AS sum_retention_time_squared,
        SUM(importance_score * importance_score) OVER () AS sum_importance_score_squared
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE 
        row_retention_time IS NOT NULL AND 
        importance_score IS NOT NULL
),
correlation_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        total_count,
        sum_retention_time,
        sum_importance_score,
        sum_product,
        sum_retention_time_squared,
        sum_importance_score_squared,
        (total_count * sum_product - sum_retention_time * sum_importance_score) AS numerator,
        SQRT((total_count * sum_retention_time_squared - sum_retention_time * sum_retention_time) * 
              (total_count * sum_importance_score_squared - sum_importance_score * sum_importance_score)) AS denominator
    FROM 
        correlation_data
),
correlation_coefficient AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN denominator = 0 THEN 0 
            ELSE ROUND(numerator / denominator, 2) 
        END AS r
    FROM 
        correlation_stats
),
p_value_calculation AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN ABS(r) >= 0.5 AND p_value < 0.05 THEN 'linear'
            WHEN ABS(r) < 0.5 AND p_value < 0.05 THEN 'nonlinear'
            ELSE 'none'
        END AS relationship_type,
        r,
        1 - (0.5 * (1 + r)) AS p_value -- simplified p-value calculation for demonstration
    FROM 
        correlation_coefficient
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(p_value, 4) AS p_value,
    relationship_type,
    r
FROM 
    p_value_calculation;

WITH valid_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(row_retention_time, 0) AS row_retention_time,
        COALESCE(importance_score, 0) AS importance_score
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE 
        row_retention_time IS NOT NULL 
        AND importance_score IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(row_retention_time) AS sum_x,
        SUM(importance_score) AS sum_y,
        SUM(row_retention_time * importance_score) AS sum_xy,
        SUM(row_retention_time * row_retention_time) AS sum_xx,
        SUM(importance_score * importance_score) AS sum_yy
    FROM 
        valid_data
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        (n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / (SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y))) AS r,
        ROUND(2 * (1 - ABS((n - 2) * (n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / (SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y)))), 2) AS p_value
    FROM 
        stats
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(COALESCE(p_value, 1), 4) AS p_value,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(r) >= 0.5 THEN 'linear'
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(r) < 0.5 THEN 'nonlinear'
        ELSE 'none'
    END AS relationship_type,
    ROUND(COALESCE(r, 0), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM 
    correlation;

samples.question: Identify and remove any outliers in the "row retention time" column using the Z-score method with a Z-score threshold of 3. Provide the number of removed outliers.
samples.constraints: Use the Z-score method to identify outliers in the "row retention time" column. Any data point with a Z-score greater than 3 or less than -3 is considered an outlier and should be removed.

variants.sql:
WITH retention_time_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(row_retention_time) AS avg_retention_time,
        STDDEV(row_retention_time) AS stddev_retention_time
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
),
outlier_detection AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        (row_retention_time - (SELECT avg_retention_time FROM retention_time_stats)) / 
        (SELECT stddev_retention_time FROM retention_time_stats) AS z_score
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
)
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) AS removed_outliers_count
FROM 
    outlier_detection
WHERE 
    z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3;

SELECT COUNT(*) AS removed_outliers_count
FROM (
    SELECT row_retention_time,
           (row_retention_time - AVG(row_retention_time) OVER ()) / NULLIF(STDDEV(row_retention_time) OVER (), 0) AS z_score
    FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
) AS subquery
WHERE z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(row_retention_time) AS mean,
        STDDEV(row_retention_time) AS stddev
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
),
z_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        (row_retention_time - (SELECT mean FROM stats)) / (SELECT stddev FROM stats) AS z_score
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
)
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) AS removed_outliers_count
FROM 
    z_scores
WHERE 
    z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3;

WITH retention_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(row_retention_time) AS avg_retention_time,
        STDDEV(row_retention_time) AS stddev_retention_time
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE 
        row_retention_time IS NOT NULL
), 
outlier_identification AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS outlier_count
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome m
    JOIN 
        retention_stats r ON 1=1
    WHERE 
        m.row_retention_time IS NOT NULL 
        AND (ABS(m.row_retention_time - r.avg_retention_time) / NULLIF(r.stddev_retention_time, 0)) > 3
)
SELECT 
    COALESCE(outlier_count, 0) AS removed_outliers_count
FROM 
    outlier_identification;

samples.question: Explore the distribution of the "importance.score" column and determine if it follows a normal distribution by conducting a Shapiro-Wilk test. If the p-value is less than 0.05, apply a log transformation to make the distribution closer to normal. Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the transformed "importance.score" column.
samples.constraints:
1. Use the Shapiro-Wilk test to determine the normality of the data in the "importance.score" column. The null hypothesis for this test is that the data was drawn from a normal distribution.
2. Use a significance level of 0.05 for the Shapiro-Wilk test.
3. If the p-value from the Shapiro-Wilk test is less than 0.05, apply a natural log transformation to the "importance.score" column.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE importance.score IS NOT NULL) > 0 THEN 0.0 
        ELSE 1.0 
    END AS is_normal,
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN importance.score IS NOT NULL THEN LOG(importance.score) END), 2) AS mean_log_importance_score,
    ROUND(STDEV(CASE WHEN importance.score IS NOT NULL THEN LOG(importance.score) END), 2) AS stddev_log_importance_score
FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome;

WITH importance_scores AS (
    SELECT importance.score
    FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
),
shapiro_test AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN COUNT(*) > 0 THEN 0.0 
            ELSE 1.0 
        END AS is_normal,
        ROUND(AVG(importance.score), 2) AS mean_score,
        ROUND(STDDEV(importance.score), 2) AS stddev_score
    FROM importance_scores
    WHERE importance.score IS NOT NULL
),
log_transformed AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(LOG(importance.score)), 2) AS log_mean_score,
        ROUND(STDDEV(LOG(importance.score)), 2) AS log_stddev_score
    FROM importance_scores
    WHERE importance.score IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    (SELECT is_normal FROM shapiro_test) AS is_normal,
    (SELECT log_mean_score FROM log_transformed) AS log_mean_score,
    (SELECT log_stddev_score FROM log_transformed) AS log_stddev_score;

SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT p_value FROM (SELECT ROUND(SHAPIRO_WILK(importance.score), 4) AS p_value FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome)) < 0.05 
        THEN 0.0 
        ELSE 1.0 
    END AS is_normal,
    (SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(CASE 
            WHEN (SELECT p_value FROM (SELECT ROUND(SHAPIRO_WILK(importance.score), 4) AS p_value FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome)) < 0.05 
            THEN LOG(importance.score) 
            ELSE importance.score 
        END), 2) 
     FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome) AS mean_transformed,
    (SELECT 
        ROUND(STDDEV(CASE 
            WHEN (SELECT p_value FROM (SELECT ROUND(SHAPIRO_WILK(importance.score), 4) AS p_value FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome)) < 0.05 
            THEN LOG(importance.score) 
            ELSE importance.score 
        END), 2) 
     FROM imp.score.ldlr.metabolome) AS stddev_transformed;

WITH score_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        importance.score,
        ROUND(AVG(importance.score), 2) OVER () AS mean_score,
        ROUND(STDDEV(importance.score), 2) OVER () AS stddev_score,
        COUNT(*) OVER () AS total_count
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
),
shapiro_test AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN ROUND(AVG(importance.score), 2) IS NOT NULL AND total_count >= 3 THEN 0.0
            ELSE 1.0
        END AS is_normal
    FROM 
        score_stats
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN is_normal = 0.0 THEN 
            ROUND(AVG(LOG(importance.score)), 2) OVER () 
        ELSE 
            ROUND(AVG(importance.score), 2) OVER () 
    END AS transformed_mean,
    CASE 
        WHEN is_normal = 0.0 THEN 
            ROUND(STDDEV(LOG(importance.score)), 2) OVER () 
        ELSE 
            ROUND(STDDEV(importance.score), 2) OVER () 
    END AS transformed_stddev
FROM 
    shapiro_test;

WITH importance_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(importance.score, 0) AS importance_score
    FROM 
        imp.score.ldlr.metabolome
    WHERE 
        importance.score IS NOT NULL
),
shapiro_test AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        AVG(importance_score) AS mean_score,
        ROUND(STDEV(importance_score), 2) AS stddev_score
    FROM 
        importance_scores
),
normality_check AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN COUNT(*) < 3 THEN 1
            ELSE 0
        END AS is_normal
    FROM 
        importance_scores
    WHERE 
        importance_score IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    COALESCE(NULLIF(n, 0), 1) AS is_normal
FROM 
    shapiro_test, normality_check;

samples.question: What is the mean number of cases recorded across all countries and years?
samples.constraints: Calculate the mean of the column 'No. of cases'. Convert the data type of 'No. of cases' column from Object (string) to Int64 before performing calculations. Ignore those records where 'No. of cases' column value is Null or empty.

variants.sql:
WITH cleaned_data AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        CAST(REPLACE(No. of cases, '[', '') AS INTEGER) AS cases
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
    WHERE 
        No. of cases IS NOT NULL AND No. of cases <> ''
),
mean_calculation AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(cases), 2) AS mean_cases
    FROM 
        cleaned_data
)
SELECT 
    mean_cases
FROM 
    mean_calculation;

WITH cases_converted AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        CAST(NULLIF(TRIM(SUBSTR("No. of cases", 1, INSTR("No. of cases", '[') - 1)), '') AS INT) AS No_of_cases
    FROM estimated_numbers
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(No_of_cases), 2) AS mean_cases
FROM cases_converted
WHERE No_of_cases IS NOT NULL;

WITH cleaned_data AS (
    SELECT 
        CAST(NULLIF(TRIM(SUBSTR("No. of cases", 1, INSTR("No. of cases", '[') - 1)), '') AS INTEGER) AS cases
    FROM estimated_numbers
    WHERE "No. of cases" IS NOT NULL AND TRIM("No. of cases") <> ''
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(cases, 0)), 2) AS mean_cases
FROM cleaned_data;

samples.question: Which country has the highest number of deaths recorded in a single year?
samples.constraints: Calculate the maximum value in the 'No. of deaths' column. Convert the data type of 'No. of deaths' column from Object (string) to Int64 before performing calculations. Ignore those records where 'No. of deaths' column value is Null or empty. Identify the corresponding country and year for the highest number of deaths.

variants.sql:
SELECT Country, Year, MAX(CAST(SUBSTR(No. of deaths, 1, INSTR(No. of deaths, '[') - 1) AS INTEGER)) AS max_deaths
FROM estimated_numbers
WHERE No. of deaths IS NOT NULL AND No. of deaths != ''
GROUP BY Country, Year
ORDER BY max_deaths DESC
LIMIT 1;

WITH cleaned_data AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        CAST(SUBSTR(No. of deaths, 1, INSTR(No. of deaths, '[') - 1) AS INTEGER) AS deaths
    FROM estimated_numbers
    WHERE No. of deaths IS NOT NULL AND No. of deaths <> ''
),
max_deaths AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        deaths,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY deaths DESC) AS rn
    FROM cleaned_data
)
SELECT 
    Country AS max_deaths_country,
    Year AS max_deaths_year
FROM max_deaths
WHERE rn = 1;

SELECT Country, Year 
FROM estimated_numbers 
WHERE No. of deaths IS NOT NULL AND No. of deaths <> '' 
ORDER BY CAST(SUBSTR(No. of deaths, 1, INSTR(No. of deaths, '[') - 1) AS INTEGER) DESC 
LIMIT 1;

WITH cleaned_data AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        CAST(SUBSTR(No. of deaths, 1, INSTR(No. of deaths, '[') - 1) AS INTEGER) AS deaths
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
    WHERE 
        No. of deaths IS NOT NULL AND No. of deaths <> ''
),
ranked_deaths AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        deaths,
        RANK() OVER (ORDER BY deaths DESC) AS death_rank
    FROM 
        cleaned_data
)
SELECT 
    Country AS max_deaths_country,
    Year AS max_deaths_year
FROM 
    ranked_deaths
WHERE 
    death_rank = 1;

WITH cleaned_data AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        CAST(NULLIF(TRIM(SUBSTR("No. of deaths", 1, INSTR("No. of deaths", '[') - 1)), '') AS INT) AS deaths
    FROM estimated_numbers
    WHERE "No. of deaths" IS NOT NULL AND TRIM("No. of deaths") <> ''
)
SELECT 
    Country AS max_deaths_country,
    Year AS max_deaths_year,
    MAX(deaths) AS max_deaths
FROM cleaned_data
WHERE deaths IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY Country, Year
ORDER BY max_deaths DESC
LIMIT 1;

samples.question: Is there a correlation between the number of cases and the number of deaths recorded?
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient (r) to assess the strength and direction of the linear relationship between number of cases and number of deaths. Convert the data types of 'No. of cases' and 'No. of deaths' column from Object (String) to Int64 before performing calculations. Do this for complete data rather than specific country or year.

variants.sql:
SELECT ROUND(CORR(CAST(REPLACE("No. of cases", '[', '') AS INTEGER), CAST(REPLACE("No. of deaths", '[', '') AS INTEGER)), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM estimated_numbers;

WITH converted_data AS (
    SELECT 
        CAST(REPLACE("No. of cases", '[', '') AS INTEGER) AS cases,
        CAST(REPLACE("No. of deaths", '[', '') AS INTEGER) AS deaths
    FROM estimated_numbers
    WHERE "No. of cases" IS NOT NULL AND "No. of deaths" IS NOT NULL
),
mean_values AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(cases) AS mean_cases,
        AVG(deaths) AS mean_deaths
    FROM converted_data
),
covariance AS (
    SELECT 
        SUM((cases - mv.mean_cases) * (deaths - mv.mean_deaths)) / COUNT(*) AS cov
    FROM converted_data
    CROSS JOIN mean_values mv
),
variance_cases AS (
    SELECT 
        SUM(POWER(cases - mv.mean_cases, 2)) / COUNT(*) AS var_cases
    FROM converted_data
    CROSS JOIN mean_values mv
),
variance_deaths AS (
    SELECT 
        SUM(POWER(deaths - mv.mean_deaths, 2)) / COUNT(*) AS var_deaths
    FROM converted_data
    CROSS JOIN mean_values mv
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(cov.cov / (SQRT(vc.var_cases) * SQRT(vd.var_deaths)), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM covariance cov, variance_cases vc, variance_deaths vd;

SELECT ROUND(CORR(CAST(SUBSTR(No_of_cases, 1, INSTR(No_of_cases, '[') - 1) AS INTEGER), 
                   CAST(SUBSTR(No_of_deaths, 1, INSTR(No_of_deaths, '[') - 1) AS INTEGER)), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM estimated_numbers
WHERE No_of_cases IS NOT NULL AND No_of_deaths IS NOT NULL;

WITH converted_data AS (
    SELECT 
        CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE("No. of cases", '[', ''), ']', '') AS INTEGER) AS no_of_cases,
        CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE("No. of deaths", '[', ''), ']', '') AS INTEGER) AS no_of_deaths
    FROM estimated_numbers
    WHERE "No. of cases" IS NOT NULL AND "No. of deaths" IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(no_of_cases) AS sum_x,
        SUM(no_of_deaths) AS sum_y,
        SUM(no_of_cases * no_of_cases) AS sum_xx,
        SUM(no_of_deaths * no_of_deaths) AS sum_yy,
        SUM(no_of_cases * no_of_deaths) AS sum_xy
    FROM converted_data
)
SELECT 
    ROUND((n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / 
    (SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y))), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM stats;

WITH valid_data AS (
    SELECT 
        CAST(NULLIF(REPLACE(No. of cases, ']', ''), '') AS INTEGER) AS cases,
        CAST(NULLIF(REPLACE(No. of deaths, ']', ''), '') AS INTEGER) AS deaths
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
    WHERE 
        No. of cases IS NOT NULL AND No. of deaths IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(cases) AS sum_cases,
        SUM(deaths) AS sum_deaths,
        SUM(cases * cases) AS sum_cases_squared,
        SUM(deaths * deaths) AS sum_deaths_squared,
        SUM(cases * deaths) AS sum_cases_deaths
    FROM 
        valid_data
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * sum_cases_deaths - sum_cases * sum_deaths) / 
        (SQRT(n * sum_cases_squared - sum_cases * sum_cases) * SQRT(n * sum_deaths_squared - sum_deaths * sum_deaths)) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        stats
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM 
    correlation;

samples.question: What is the percentage of missing values in the "No. of cases_min" column? How does this percentage compare to the percentage of missing values in the "No. of deaths_max" column?
samples.constraints: Calculate the percentage of missing values for both "No. of cases_min" and "No. of deaths_max" column. Report the exact percentage values.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND((SUM(CASE WHEN "No. of cases_min" IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) * 100.0) / COUNT(*), 2) AS percentage_cases_min,
    ROUND((SUM(CASE WHEN "No. of deaths_max" IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) * 100.0) / COUNT(*), 2) AS percentage_deaths_max
FROM estimated_numbers;

WITH missing_values AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(CASE WHEN "No. of cases_min" IS NULL THEN 1 END) AS missing_cases_min,
        COUNT(CASE WHEN "No. of deaths_max" IS NULL THEN 1 END) AS missing_deaths_max,
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows
    FROM estimated_numbers
),
percentages AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND((missing_cases_min * 100.0 / total_rows), 2) AS percentage_cases_min,
        ROUND((missing_deaths_max * 100.0 / total_rows), 2) AS percentage_deaths_max
    FROM missing_values
)
SELECT 
    percentage_cases_min,
    percentage_deaths_max
FROM percentages;

SELECT 
    ROUND((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM estimated_numbers WHERE "No. of cases_min" IS NULL) * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 2) AS percentage_cases_min,
    ROUND((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM estimated_numbers WHERE "No. of deaths_max" IS NULL) * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 2) AS percentage_deaths_max
FROM estimated_numbers;

WITH missing_values AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(CASE WHEN "No. of cases_min" IS NULL THEN 1 END) AS missing_cases_min,
        COUNT(CASE WHEN "No. of deaths_max" IS NULL THEN 1 END) AS missing_deaths_max,
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows
    FROM estimated_numbers
)
SELECT 
    ROUND((missing_cases_min * 100.0 / total_rows), 2) AS percentage_cases_min,
    ROUND((missing_deaths_max * 100.0 / total_rows), 2) AS percentage_deaths_max
FROM missing_values;

WITH missing_values AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
        SUM(CASE WHEN No. of cases_min IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS missing_cases_min,
        SUM(CASE WHEN No. of deaths_max IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS missing_deaths_max
    FROM estimated_numbers
    WHERE Country IS NOT NULL AND Year IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(COALESCE(missing_cases_min, 0) * 100.0 / NULLIF(total_rows, 0), 2) AS percentage_cases_min,
    ROUND(COALESCE(missing_deaths_max, 0) * 100.0 / NULLIF(total_rows, 0), 2) AS percentage_deaths_max
FROM missing_values;

samples.question: Among the countries in the "Americas" region, which country has the highest average number of cases recorded over the years?
samples.constraints: Calculate the average of "No. of cases" for each country in the "Americas" region and report the country with the highest average number of cases. Count only complete years, i.e., exclude years with missing data.

variants.sql:
SELECT Country, ROUND(AVG(CAST(REPLACE("No. of cases", '[', '') AS INTEGER)), 2) AS avg_cases
FROM estimated_numbers
WHERE "WHO Region" = 'Americas' AND "No. of cases" IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY Country
ORDER BY avg_cases DESC
LIMIT 1;

WITH cases_data AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        CAST(REPLACE(No. of cases, '[', '') AS INTEGER) AS cases
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
    WHERE 
        "WHO Region" = 'Americas' AND 
        No. of cases IS NOT NULL AND 
        No. of cases <> ''
),
average_cases AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        AVG(cases) AS avg_cases
    FROM 
        cases_data
    GROUP BY 
        Country
)
SELECT 
    Country AS country_name
FROM 
    average_cases
ORDER BY 
    avg_cases DESC
LIMIT 1;

SELECT Country 
FROM (
    SELECT Country, 
           AVG(CAST(REPLACE(No. of cases, '[', '') AS FLOAT)) AS avg_cases
    FROM estimated_numbers
    WHERE "WHO Region" = 'Americas' 
      AND No. of cases IS NOT NULL 
      AND No. of cases <> ''
    GROUP BY Country
) 
ORDER BY avg_cases DESC 
LIMIT 1;

WITH country_cases AS (
    SELECT 
        Country, 
        AVG(CAST(REPLACE(SUBSTR("No. of cases", 1, INSTR("No. of cases", '[') - 1), ',', '') AS FLOAT)) AS avg_cases
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
    WHERE 
        "WHO Region" = 'Americas' AND 
        "No. of cases" IS NOT NULL AND 
        "No. of cases" != ''
    GROUP BY 
        Country
)
SELECT 
    Country 
FROM 
    country_cases
ORDER BY 
    avg_cases DESC
LIMIT 1;

WITH parsed_cases AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Year,
        CAST(NULLIF(REPLACE("No. of cases", '[', ''), '') AS FLOAT) AS No_of_cases
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
    WHERE 
        "WHO Region" = 'Americas' 
        AND "No. of cases" IS NOT NULL
        AND "No. of cases" <> ''
),
average_cases AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        ROUND(AVG(No_of_cases), 2) AS avg_cases
    FROM 
        parsed_cases
    GROUP BY 
        Country
)
SELECT 
    Country
FROM 
    average_cases
ORDER BY 
    avg_cases DESC
LIMIT 1;

samples.question: Are there any outliers in the "No. of deaths_max" column for each country? How do these outliers affect the overall distribution of recorded deaths?
samples.constraints: Use the IQR method (1.5*IQR rule) to detect the outliers. If there are any outliers, remove them and then recalculate the mean number of deaths.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN deaths_max < lower_bound OR deaths_max > upper_bound THEN NULL ELSE deaths_max END), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers,
    ROUND(AVG(deaths_max), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers
FROM (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        deaths_max,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) AS q3,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) AS q1,
        (PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) - PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country)) * 1.5 AS iqr,
        (PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) - ((PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) - PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country)) * 1.5)) AS lower_bound,
        (PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) + ((PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) - PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) OVER (PARTITION BY Country)) * 1.5)) AS upper_bound
    FROM (
        SELECT 
            Country,
            CAST(SUBSTR(No. of deaths_max, 1, INSTR(No. of deaths_max, '[') - 1) AS FLOAT) AS deaths_max
        FROM estimated_numbers
    )
) AS subquery;

WITH death_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        CAST(REPLACE(No. of deaths_max, '[', '') AS FLOAT) AS deaths_max,
        CAST(REPLACE(No. of deaths_min, '[', '') AS FLOAT) AS deaths_min,
        CAST(No. of deaths_median AS INT) AS deaths_median
    FROM estimated_numbers
),
quartiles AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_median) AS Q1,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_median) AS Q3
    FROM death_stats
    GROUP BY Country
),
iqr AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Q1,
        Q3,
        (Q3 - Q1) AS IQR
    FROM quartiles
),
outlier_bounds AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Q1 - 1.5 * IQR AS lower_bound,
        Q3 + 1.5 * IQR AS upper_bound
    FROM iqr
),
deaths_with_outliers AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(deaths_median) AS mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers
    FROM death_stats
),
deaths_without_outliers AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(deaths_median) AS mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers
    FROM death_stats d
    JOIN outlier_bounds ob ON d.Country = ob.Country
    WHERE d.deaths_median BETWEEN ob.lower_bound AND ob.upper_bound
)
SELECT 
    ROUND((SELECT mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers FROM deaths_with_outliers), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers,
    ROUND((SELECT mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers FROM deaths_without_outliers), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers;

SELECT 
    ROUND((SELECT AVG(CAST(No_of_deaths_median AS FLOAT)) 
            FROM estimated_numbers) , 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers,
    ROUND((SELECT AVG(CAST(No_of_deaths_median AS FLOAT)) 
            FROM estimated_numbers 
            WHERE CAST(No_of_deaths_median AS FLOAT) NOT IN (
                SELECT No_of_deaths_median 
                FROM (
                    SELECT No_of_deaths_median,
                           (PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) OVER()) - PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) OVER() AS IQR,
                           PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) OVER() - 1.5 * ((PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) OVER()) - PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) OVER()) AS lower_bound,
                           PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) OVER() + 1.5 * ((PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) OVER()) - PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) OVER()) AS upper_bound
                    FROM estimated_numbers
                ) AS bounds
                WHERE No_of_deaths_median < lower_bound OR No_of_deaths_median > upper_bound
            )
    ), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers
FROM estimated_numbers;

WITH death_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        No. of deaths_median,
        No. of deaths_min,
        No. of deaths_max,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY CAST(SUBSTR("No. of deaths_max", 1, INSTR("No. of deaths_max", '[') - 1) AS FLOAT)) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) AS Q1,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY CAST(SUBSTR("No. of deaths_max", 1, INSTR("No. of deaths_max", '[') - 1) AS FLOAT)) OVER (PARTITION BY Country) AS Q3
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
),
iqr_calculation AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Q1,
        Q3,
        (Q3 - Q1) AS IQR,
        (Q1 - 1.5 * (Q3 - Q1)) AS lower_bound,
        (Q3 + 1.5 * (Q3 - Q1)) AS upper_bound
    FROM 
        death_stats
),
mean_deaths AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        AVG(CAST(SUBSTR("No. of deaths_max", 1, INSTR("No. of deaths_max", '[') - 1) AS FLOAT)) AS mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers,
        AVG(CASE 
            WHEN CAST(SUBSTR("No. of deaths_max", 1, INSTR("No. of deaths_max", '[') - 1) AS FLOAT) < lower_bound OR 
                 CAST(SUBSTR("No. of deaths_max", 1, INSTR("No. of deaths_max", '[') - 1) AS FLOAT) > upper_bound 
            THEN NULL 
            ELSE CAST(SUBSTR("No. of deaths_max", 1, INSTR("No. of deaths_max", '[') - 1) AS FLOAT) 
        END) AS mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
    JOIN 
        iqr_calculation USING (Country)
    GROUP BY 
        Country
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers,
    ROUND(AVG(mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers
FROM 
    mean_deaths;

WITH death_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        CAST(COALESCE(NULLIF(No. of deaths_max, ''), '0') AS FLOAT) AS deaths_max
    FROM 
        estimated_numbers
    WHERE 
        No. of deaths_max IS NOT NULL
),
quantiles AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        ROUND(AVG(deaths_max), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) AS Q1,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY deaths_max) AS Q3
    FROM 
        death_stats
    GROUP BY 
        Country
),
outlier_thresholds AS (
    SELECT 
        Country,
        Q1,
        Q3,
        (Q3 - Q1) * 1.5 AS IQR,
        (Q1 - ((Q3 - Q1) * 1.5)) AS lower_bound,
        (Q3 + ((Q3 - Q1) * 1.5)) AS upper_bound
    FROM 
        quantiles
),
filtered_deaths AS (
    SELECT 
        ds.Country,
        ds.deaths_max
    FROM 
        death_stats ds
    JOIN 
        outlier_thresholds ot ON ds.Country = ot.Country
    WHERE 
        ds.deaths_max IS NOT NULL 
        AND ds.deaths_max >= ot.lower_bound 
        AND ds.deaths_max <= ot.upper_bound
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(ds.deaths_max), 2) AS mean_no_of_deaths_without_outliers,
    q.mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers
FROM 
    filtered_deaths ds
JOIN 
    quantiles q ON ds.Country = q.Country
GROUP BY 
    q.mean_no_of_deaths_with_outliers;

samples.question: Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the wage column.
samples.constraints: The mean and standard deviation of the wage should be calculated using pandas' `mean()` and `std()` methods respectively. Do not apply any transformations, filtering or alteration to the wage data.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND(AVG(wage), 2) AS mean_wage, ROUND(STDDEV(wage), 2) AS std_wage FROM "beauty and the labor market";

WITH wage_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(wage) AS mean_wage,
        STDDEV(wage) AS std_wage
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(mean_wage, 2) AS mean_wage,
    ROUND(std_wage, 2) AS std_wage
FROM 
    wage_stats;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(wage), 2) AS mean_wage, 
    ROUND(SQRT(AVG((wage - (SELECT AVG(wage) FROM "beauty and the labor market")) * (wage - (SELECT AVG(wage) FROM "beauty and the labor market")))), 2) AS std_wage
FROM "beauty and the labor market";

WITH wage_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(wage) OVER () , 2) AS mean_wage,
        ROUND(STDDEV(wage) OVER (), 2) AS std_wage
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
)
SELECT 
    mean_wage, 
    std_wage 
FROM 
    wage_stats 
LIMIT 1;

WITH wage_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(wage, 0)), 2) AS mean_wage,
        ROUND(SQRT(AVG(POWER(NULLIF(wage, 0) - ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(wage, 0)), 2), 2)), 1), 2) AS std_wage
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
    WHERE 
        wage IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    mean_wage, 
    std_wage 
FROM 
    wage_stats;

samples.question: Calculate the correlation between the wage column and the exper column.
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient (r) to assess the strength and direction of the linear relationship between wage and the exper. Assess the significance of the correlation using a two-tailed test with a significance level (alpha) of 0.05. Report the p-value associated with the correlation test. Consider the relationship to be linear if the p-value is less than 0.05 and the absolute value of r is greater than or equal to 0.5. Consider the relationship to be nonlinear if the p-value is less than 0.05 and the absolute value of r is less than 0.5. If the p-value is greater than or equal to 0.05, report that there is no significant correlation.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(CORR(wage, exper), 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN ABS(CORR(wage, exper)) >= 0.5 AND PVAL(CORR(wage, exper)) < 0.05 THEN 'linear'
        WHEN ABS(CORR(wage, exper)) < 0.5 AND PVAL(CORR(wage, exper)) < 0.05 THEN 'nonlinear'
        ELSE 'no significant correlation'
    END AS relationship_type
FROM 
    "beauty and the labor market";

WITH correlation_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(wage) AS sum_wage,
        SUM(exper) AS sum_exper,
        SUM(wage * exper) AS sum_wage_exper,
        SUM(wage * wage) AS sum_wage_sq,
        SUM(exper * exper) AS sum_exper_sq
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
),
correlation_calculation AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        sum_wage,
        sum_exper,
        sum_wage_exper,
        sum_wage_sq,
        sum_exper_sq,
        (n * sum_wage_exper - sum_wage * sum_exper) / 
        SQRT((n * sum_wage_sq - sum_wage * sum_wage) * (n * sum_exper_sq - sum_exper * sum_exper)) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        correlation_data
),
p_value_calculation AS (
    SELECT 
        correlation_coefficient,
        2 * (1 - ABS(correlation_coefficient)) AS p_value
    FROM 
        correlation_calculation
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(correlation_coefficient) >= 0.5 THEN 'linear'
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(correlation_coefficient) < 0.5 THEN 'nonlinear'
        ELSE 'no significant correlation'
    END AS relationship_type
FROM 
    p_value_calculation;

SELECT 
    ROUND(corr, 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(corr) >= 0.5 THEN 'linear'
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(corr) < 0.5 THEN 'nonlinear'
        ELSE 'no significant correlation'
    END AS relationship_type
FROM (
    SELECT 
        (COUNT(*) * SUM(wage * exper) - SUM(wage) * SUM(exper)) / 
        (SQRT((COUNT(*) * SUM(wage * wage) - SUM(wage) * SUM(wage)) * 
               (COUNT(*) * SUM(exper * exper) - SUM(exper) * SUM(exper)))) AS corr,
        2 * (1 - (SELECT 
                      COUNT(*) - 2 
                  FROM beauty and the labor market) / 
                  (SELECT 
                      COUNT(*) 
                  FROM beauty and the labor market)) AS p_value
    FROM beauty and the labor market
) AS correlation_results;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(wage) AS sum_x,
        SUM(exper) AS sum_y,
        SUM(wage * exper) AS sum_xy,
        SUM(wage * wage) AS sum_xx,
        SUM(exper * exper) AS sum_yy
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y))) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        stats
),
p_value AS (
    SELECT 
        2 * (1 - ABS(correlation_coefficient)) AS p_value
    FROM 
        correlation
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(correlation_coefficient) >= 0.5 THEN 'linear'
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(correlation_coefficient) < 0.5 THEN 'nonlinear'
        ELSE 'no significant correlation'
    END AS relationship_type
FROM 
    correlation, p_value;

WITH correlation_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(wage) AS sum_wage,
        SUM(exper) AS sum_exper,
        SUM(wage * exper) AS sum_wage_exper,
        SUM(wage * wage) AS sum_wage_sq,
        SUM(exper * exper) AS sum_exper_sq
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
    WHERE 
        wage IS NOT NULL AND exper IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        sum_wage,
        sum_exper,
        sum_wage_exper,
        sum_wage_sq,
        sum_exper_sq,
        ROUND((n * sum_wage_exper - sum_wage * sum_exper) / 
              NULLIF(SQRT((n * sum_wage_sq - sum_wage * sum_wage) * (n * sum_exper_sq - sum_exper * sum_exper)), 0), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        correlation_data
),
p_value AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(2 * (1 - ABS(correlation_coefficient)), 4) AS p_value
    FROM 
        stats
)
SELECT 
    COALESCE(correlation_coefficient, 0) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(correlation_coefficient) >= 0.5 THEN 'linear'
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 THEN 'nonlinear'
        ELSE 'no significant correlation'
    END AS relationship_type
FROM 
    stats, p_value;

samples.question: Perform feature engineering by creating a new feature called "experience_score" that is calculated by multiplying the exper column with the looks column. Then, calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient between the "experience_score" feature and the wage column.
samples.constraints: Create "experience_score" by directly multiplying values of exper and looks column. Calculate Pearson correlation coefficient between the new feature "experience_score" and wage. Correlation should be calculated up to three decimal places.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND(CORR(exper * looks, wage), 3) AS correlation FROM "beauty and the labor market";

WITH experience_data AS (
    SELECT 
        wage,
        exper * looks AS experience_score
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(wage) AS sum_wage,
        SUM(experience_score) AS sum_experience_score,
        SUM(wage * experience_score) AS sum_product,
        SUM(wage * wage) AS sum_wage_sq,
        SUM(experience_score * experience_score) AS sum_experience_score_sq
    FROM 
        experience_data
)
SELECT 
    ROUND((n * sum_product - sum_wage * sum_experience_score) / 
    (SQRT((n * sum_wage_sq - sum_wage * sum_wage) * 
    (n * sum_experience_score_sq - sum_experience_score * sum_experience_score))), 3) AS correlation
FROM 
    stats;

WITH experience_data AS (
    SELECT 
        wage,
        exper * looks AS experience_score
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(experience_score) AS sum_x,
        SUM(wage) AS sum_y,
        SUM(experience_score * wage) AS sum_xy,
        SUM(experience_score * experience_score) AS sum_xx,
        SUM(wage * wage) AS sum_yy
    FROM 
        experience_data
)
SELECT 
    ROUND((n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / 
    (SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y))), 3) AS correlation
FROM 
    stats;

WITH experience_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(exper, 0) * COALESCE(looks, 0) AS experience_score,
        COALESCE(wage, 0) AS wage
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
    WHERE 
        exper IS NOT NULL AND 
        looks IS NOT NULL AND 
        wage IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(CORR(experience_score, wage), 3) AS correlation
FROM 
    experience_data;

samples.question: Perform machine learning by training a linear regression model to predict the wage based on the features exper, looks, union, goodhlth, black, female, married, south, bigcity, smllcity, service, and educ. Use the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for evaluating the model's performance.
samples.constraints: Perform the machine learning task using the sklearn library's LinearRegression() function. Split the dataset into a 70% training set and a 30% test set. Set the random seed to 42 for reproducibility of the results. Calculate the RMSE on the test set.

variants.sql:
SELECT ROUND(SQRT(AVG(POWER(wage - predicted_wage, 2))), 2) AS RMSE
FROM (
    SELECT wage, 
           (0.5 * exper + 0.3 * looks + 0.1 * union + 0.1 * goodhlth + 0.1 * black + 0.1 * female + 0.1 * married + 0.1 * south + 0.1 * bigcity + 0.1 * smllcity + 0.1 * service + 0.1 * educ) AS predicted_wage
    FROM "beauty and the labor market"
    ORDER BY RANDOM()
    LIMIT (SELECT COUNT(*) * 0.3 FROM "beauty and the labor market")
) AS predictions;

WITH training_set AS (
    SELECT wage, exper, looks, union, goodhlth, black, female, married, south, bigcity, smllcity, service, educ
    FROM "beauty and the labor market"
    ORDER BY RANDOM()
    LIMIT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM "beauty and the labor market") * 0.7
),
test_set AS (
    SELECT wage, exper, looks, union, goodhlth, black, female, married, south, bigcity, smllcity, service, educ
    FROM "beauty and the labor market"
    WHERE (exper, looks, union, goodhlth, black, female, married, south, bigcity, smllcity, service, educ) NOT IN (
        SELECT exper, looks, union, goodhlth, black, female, married, south, bigcity, smllcity, service, educ
        FROM training_set
    )
),
predictions AS (
    SELECT 
        wage,
        ROUND(SQRT(AVG(POWER(wage - predicted_wage, 2))), 2) AS rmse
    FROM (
        SELECT 
            wage,
            (0.5 * exper + 0.5 * looks + 0.5 * union + 0.5 * goodhlth + 0.5 * black + 0.5 * female + 0.5 * married + 0.5 * south + 0.5 * bigcity + 0.5 * smllcity + 0.5 * service + 0.5 * educ) AS predicted_wage
        FROM test_set
    )
)
SELECT rmse FROM predictions;

SELECT ROUND(SQRT(AVG(POW(predicted_wage - actual_wage, 2))), 2) AS RMSE
FROM (
    SELECT wage AS actual_wage,
           (SELECT AVG(wage) FROM "beauty and the labor market" 
            WHERE RANDOM() < 0.3) AS predicted_wage
    FROM "beauty and the labor market"
    WHERE RANDOM() >= 0.3
) AS subquery;

SELECT ROUND(SQRT(AVG(POWER(wage - predicted_wage, 2))), 2) AS RMSE
FROM (
    SELECT wage, 
           AVG(wage) OVER (PARTITION BY group_id) AS predicted_wage,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY RANDOM()) AS row_num,
           COUNT(*) OVER () AS total_count,
           CASE WHEN ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY RANDOM()) <= 0.3 * COUNT(*) OVER () THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS group_id
    FROM `beauty and the labor market`
) AS subquery
WHERE group_id = 1;

WITH training_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(exper, 0) AS exper,
        COALESCE(looks, 0) AS looks,
        COALESCE(union, 0) AS union,
        COALESCE(goodhlth, 0) AS goodhlth,
        COALESCE(black, 0) AS black,
        COALESCE(female, 0) AS female,
        COALESCE(married, 0) AS married,
        COALESCE(south, 0) AS south,
        COALESCE(bigcity, 0) AS bigcity,
        COALESCE(smllcity, 0) AS smllcity,
        COALESCE(service, 0) AS service,
        COALESCE(educ, 0) AS educ,
        COALESCE(wage, 0) AS wage
    FROM 
        "beauty and the labor market"
    WHERE 
        wage IS NOT NULL AND 
        exper IS NOT NULL AND 
        looks IS NOT NULL AND 
        union IS NOT NULL AND 
        goodhlth IS NOT NULL AND 
        black IS NOT NULL AND 
        female IS NOT NULL AND 
        married IS NOT NULL AND 
        south IS NOT NULL AND 
        bigcity IS NOT NULL AND 
        smllcity IS NOT NULL AND 
        service IS NOT NULL AND 
        educ IS NOT NULL
),
train_test_split AS (
    SELECT *, 
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY RANDOM()) AS rn
    FROM training_data
),
train AS (
    SELECT * 
    FROM train_test_split 
    WHERE rn <= (SELECT COUNT(*) * 0.7 FROM train_test_split)
),
test AS (
    SELECT * 
    FROM train_test_split 
    WHERE rn > (SELECT COUNT(*) * 0.7 FROM train_test_split)
),
predictions AS (
    SELECT 
        t.wage AS actual_wage,
        ROUND(POWER(COALESCE(t.wage, 0) - COALESCE(p.predicted_wage, 0), 2), 0) AS squared_error
    FROM 
        test t
    LEFT JOIN 
        (SELECT 
            AVG(wage) AS predicted_wage
         FROM 
            train) p ON 1=1
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(SQRT(SUM(squared_error) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0)), 2) AS RMSE
FROM 
    predictions;

samples.question: Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the "Volume" column.
samples.constraints: Use the built-in functions in Python's pandas library for computation. Round the result to 2 decimal places.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND(AVG(Volume), 2) AS mean_volume, ROUND(STDEV(Volume), 2) AS std_dev_volume FROM microsoft;

WITH volume_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(Volume) AS mean_volume,
        STDDEV(Volume) AS std_dev_volume
    FROM microsoft
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(mean_volume, 2) AS mean_volume,
    ROUND(std_dev_volume, 2) AS std_dev_volume
FROM volume_stats;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(Volume), 2) AS mean_volume, 
    ROUND(STDDEV(Volume), 2) AS std_dev_volume 
FROM 
    (SELECT Volume FROM microsoft);

WITH volume_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(Volume) OVER (), 2) AS mean_volume,
        ROUND(STDDEV(Volume) OVER (), 2) AS std_dev_volume
    FROM microsoft
)
SELECT DISTINCT mean_volume, std_dev_volume FROM volume_stats;

WITH volume_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(AVG(COALESCE(Volume, 0)), 0) AS mean_volume,
        COALESCE(STDEV(COALESCE(Volume, 0)), 0) AS std_dev_volume
    FROM microsoft
    WHERE Volume IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(mean_volume, 2) AS mean_volume,
    ROUND(std_dev_volume, 2) AS std_dev_volume
FROM volume_stats;

samples.question: Check if the "Close" column adheres to a normal distribution.
samples.constraints: Use the Shapiro-Wilk test to assess the normality of the "Close" column. If the p-value is less than 0.05, consider the data to be non-normally distributed. Otherwise, consider it to be normally distributed.

variants.sql:
WITH close_data AS (
    SELECT Close
    FROM microsoft
),
shapiro_test AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        AVG(Close) AS mean,
        SUM((Close - AVG(Close)) * (Close - AVG(Close))) AS variance_sum
    FROM close_data
),
normality_result AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN (n < 3) THEN 'Insufficient Data'
            ELSE 
                CASE 
                    WHEN (variance_sum / (n - 1)) < 0.05 THEN 'Non-normal'
                    ELSE 'Normal'
                END
        END AS normality_test_result
    FROM shapiro_test
)
SELECT normality_test_result
FROM normality_result;

SELECT CASE 
           WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM microsoft) < 3 THEN 'Insufficient data' 
           ELSE 
               CASE 
                   WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM microsoft WHERE Close IS NOT NULL) < 3 THEN 'Insufficient data' 
                   ELSE 
                       CASE 
                           WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM microsoft WHERE Close IS NOT NULL) > 0 THEN 
                               CASE 
                                   WHEN (SELECT ROUND((SUM((Close - (SELECT AVG(Close) FROM microsoft WHERE Close IS NOT NULL)) * (Close - (SELECT AVG(Close) FROM microsoft WHERE Close IS NOT NULL)))) / (COUNT(*) - 1), 2) 
                                   FROM microsoft WHERE Close IS NOT NULL) < 0.05 THEN 'Non-normal' 
                                   ELSE 'Normal' 
                               END 
                           ELSE 'Non-normal' 
                       END 
               END 
       END AS normality_test_result;

WITH close_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        Close,
        AVG(Close) OVER () AS mean_close,
        STDDEV(Close) OVER () AS stddev_close,
        COUNT(*) OVER () AS n
    FROM microsoft
),
normality_test AS (
    SELECT 
        SUM(POWER((Close - mean_close) / stddev_close, 2)) AS W,
        n
    FROM close_stats
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (n - 1) * W < 0.05 THEN 'Non-normal' 
        ELSE 'Normal' 
    END AS normality_test_result
FROM normality_test;

WITH close_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(Close, 0) AS CloseValue
    FROM 
        microsoft
    WHERE 
        Close IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(CloseValue), 2) AS avg_close,
        ROUND(STDDEV(CloseValue), 2) AS stddev_close,
        COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM 
        close_data
),
shapiro_test AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN COUNT(*) < 3 THEN 'Insufficient Data'
            ELSE 
                CASE 
                    WHEN ROUND(1 - (0.5 * (1 + EXP(-0.5 * POWER((avg_close - CloseValue) / stddev_close, 2)))), 2) < 0.05 THEN 'Non-normal' 
                    ELSE 'Normal' 
                END 
        END AS normality_test_result
    FROM 
        close_data, stats
)
SELECT 
    normality_test_result 
FROM 
    shapiro_test;

samples.question: Calculate the correlation coefficient between the "High" and "Low" columns.
samples.constraints: Use the Pearson correlation coefficient for computation. Round the result to 2 decimal places.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND(CORR(High, Low), 2) AS correlation_coefficient FROM microsoft;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(High) AS sum_high,
        SUM(Low) AS sum_low,
        SUM(High * High) AS sum_high_sq,
        SUM(Low * Low) AS sum_low_sq
    FROM microsoft
), correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * SUM(High * Low) - sum_high * sum_low) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_high_sq - sum_high * sum_high) * (n * sum_low_sq - sum_low * sum_low))) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM microsoft, stats
)
SELECT ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM correlation;

SELECT ROUND((SELECT COUNT(*) * SUM(High * Low) - SUM(High) * SUM(Low) 
               FROM (SELECT High, Low FROM microsoft) AS subquery) /
              (SQRT((COUNT(*) * SUM(High * High) - SUM(High) * SUM(High)) * 
                     (COUNT(*) * SUM(Low * Low) - SUM(Low) * SUM(Low)))), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM microsoft;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(High) AS sum_high,
        SUM(Low) AS sum_low,
        SUM(High * High) AS sum_high_sq,
        SUM(Low * Low) AS sum_low_sq
    FROM microsoft
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * SUM(High * Low) - sum_high * sum_low) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_high_sq - sum_high * sum_high) * (n * sum_low_sq - sum_low * sum_low))) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM microsoft, stats
)
SELECT ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM correlation;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(High) AS sum_high,
        SUM(Low) AS sum_low,
        SUM(High * Low) AS sum_high_low,
        SUM(High * High) AS sum_high_sq,
        SUM(Low * Low) AS sum_low_sq
    FROM microsoft
    WHERE High IS NOT NULL AND Low IS NOT NULL
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(n, 0) AS n,
        COALESCE(sum_high, 0) AS sum_high,
        COALESCE(sum_low, 0) AS sum_low,
        COALESCE(sum_high_low, 0) AS sum_high_low,
        COALESCE(sum_high_sq, 0) AS sum_high_sq,
        COALESCE(sum_low_sq, 0) AS sum_low_sq
    FROM stats
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(
        (n * sum_high_low - sum_high * sum_low) / 
        NULLIF(SQRT((n * sum_high_sq - sum_high * sum_high) * (n * sum_low_sq - sum_low * sum_low)), 0), 2
    ) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM correlation;

samples.question: Create a new column called "Daily Return" that calculates the percentage change in the "Close" price from the previous day. Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the "Daily Return" column.
samples.constraints: Calculate "Daily Return" as ((Close price of today - Close price of previous day) / Close price of previous day) * 100. Calculate mean and standard deviation to two decimal places.

variants.sql:
WITH daily_returns AS (
    SELECT 
        Date,
        Close,
        LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date) AS Prev_Close
    FROM 
        microsoft
),
returns_calculated AS (
    SELECT 
        Date,
        ROUND(((Close - Prev_Close) / Prev_Close) * 100, 2) AS Daily_Return
    FROM 
        daily_returns
    WHERE 
        Prev_Close IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(Daily_Return), 2) AS daily_return_mean,
    ROUND(STDDEV(Daily_Return), 2) AS daily_return_std
FROM 
    returns_calculated;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(daily_return), 2) AS daily_return_mean,
    ROUND(STDDEV(daily_return), 2) AS daily_return_std
FROM (
    SELECT 
        (Close - LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date)) / LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date) * 100 AS daily_return
    FROM microsoft
) AS daily_returns
WHERE daily_return IS NOT NULL;

WITH daily_returns AS (
    SELECT 
        Date,
        Close,
        LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date) AS Prev_Close,
        ((Close - LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date)) / LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date)) * 100 AS Daily_Return
    FROM 
        microsoft
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(Daily_Return), 2) AS daily_return_mean,
    ROUND(STDDEV(Daily_Return), 2) AS daily_return_std
FROM 
    daily_returns
WHERE 
    Prev_Close IS NOT NULL;

WITH daily_returns AS (
    SELECT 
        Date,
        Close,
        LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date) AS Prev_Close,
        CASE 
            WHEN LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date) IS NOT NULL THEN 
                ROUND(((Close - LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date)) / NULLIF(LAG(Close) OVER (ORDER BY Date), 0)) * 100, 2)
            ELSE 
                NULL 
        END AS Daily_Return
    FROM 
        microsoft
    WHERE 
        Close IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(Daily_Return, 0)), 2) AS daily_return_mean,
    ROUND(STDEV(COALESCE(Daily_Return, 0)), 2) AS daily_return_std
FROM 
    daily_returns
WHERE 
    Daily_Return IS NOT NULL;

samples.question: Perform comprehensive data preprocessing on the "Date" column to extract the month and year information. Calculate the average closing price for each month and year combination. Return the month and year combination which has the highest average closing price.
samples.constraints:
Set the start of each month to be the first day of the month and the end of the month to be the last day of the month.
The calculation of the average closing price should be done using the arithmetic mean. 
For ties, return the most recent month and year combination.

variants.sql:
SELECT strftime('%Y-%m', Date) AS Month_Year, ROUND(AVG(Close), 2) AS Avg_Close
FROM microsoft
GROUP BY Month_Year
ORDER BY Avg_Close DESC, Month_Year DESC
LIMIT 1;

WITH PreprocessedData AS (
    SELECT 
        DATE(STRFTIME('%Y-%m-%d', Date)) AS MonthYear,
        ROUND(AVG(Close), 2) AS AvgClose
    FROM 
        microsoft
    GROUP BY 
        strftime('%Y-%m', MonthYear)
),
RankedData AS (
    SELECT 
        MonthYear,
        AvgClose,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY AvgClose DESC, MonthYear DESC) AS Rank
    FROM 
        PreprocessedData
)
SELECT 
    strftime('%m', MonthYear) AS Month,
    strftime('%Y', MonthYear) AS Year,
    AvgClose AS Highest_Monthly_Average_Close_Price
FROM 
    RankedData
WHERE 
    Rank = 1;

SELECT Month_Year, ROUND(AVG(Close), 2) AS Average_Close
FROM (
    SELECT strftime('%Y-%m', Date) AS Month_Year, Close
    FROM microsoft
) AS Monthly_Data
GROUP BY Month_Year
ORDER BY Average_Close DESC, Month_Year DESC
LIMIT 1;

WITH Monthly_Averages AS (
    SELECT 
        strftime('%Y-%m', Date) AS YearMonth,
        ROUND(AVG(Close), 2) AS AvgClose
    FROM 
        microsoft
    GROUP BY 
        YearMonth
),
Ranked_Averages AS (
    SELECT 
        YearMonth,
        AvgClose,
        RANK() OVER (ORDER BY AvgClose DESC, YearMonth DESC) AS AvgRank
    FROM 
        Monthly_Averages
)
SELECT 
    YearMonth, 
    AvgClose 
FROM 
    Ranked_Averages 
WHERE 
    AvgRank = 1;

WITH PreprocessedData AS (
    SELECT 
        DATE(COALESCE(STRFTIME('%Y-%m-%d', Date), '1970-01-01')) AS MonthYear,
        ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(Close, 0)), 2) AS AvgClose
    FROM microsoft
    WHERE Close IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY strftime('%Y-%m', MonthYear)
),
RankedData AS (
    SELECT 
        strftime('%Y', MonthYear) AS Year,
        strftime('%m', MonthYear) AS Month,
        AvgClose,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY AvgClose DESC, MonthYear DESC) AS Rank
    FROM PreprocessedData
)
SELECT 
    Month, Year, AvgClose AS Highest_Monthly_Average_Close_Price
FROM RankedData
WHERE Rank = 1;

samples.question: Calculate the correlation coefficient between ApplicantIncome and LoanAmount.
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient to assess the strength and direction of linear relationship between ApplicantIncome and LoanAmount. Ignore the rows with missing values for either of the two columns. Round the correlation coefficient to two decimal places.

variants.sql:
WITH income_loan AS (
    SELECT ApplicantIncome, LoanAmount
    FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
    WHERE ApplicantIncome IS NOT NULL AND LoanAmount IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome) AS sum_x,
        SUM(LoanAmount) AS sum_y,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome * LoanAmount) AS sum_xy,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome * ApplicantIncome) AS sum_xx,
        SUM(LoanAmount * LoanAmount) AS sum_yy
    FROM income_loan
)
SELECT ROUND((n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / 
              SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y)), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM stats;

SELECT ROUND((SELECT COUNT(*) * SUM(ApplicantIncome * LoanAmount) - SUM(ApplicantIncome) * SUM(LoanAmount) 
               FROM (SELECT ApplicantIncome, LoanAmount 
                     FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN 
                     WHERE ApplicantIncome IS NOT NULL AND LoanAmount IS NOT NULL) AS subquery) 
              / (SQRT((SELECT COUNT(*) * SUM(ApplicantIncome * ApplicantIncome) - SUM(ApplicantIncome) * SUM(ApplicantIncome) 
                        FROM (SELECT ApplicantIncome, LoanAmount 
                              FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN 
                              WHERE ApplicantIncome IS NOT NULL AND LoanAmount IS NOT NULL) AS subquery1) 
                      * (SELECT COUNT(*) * SUM(LoanAmount * LoanAmount) - SUM(LoanAmount) * SUM(LoanAmount) 
                        FROM (SELECT ApplicantIncome, LoanAmount 
                              FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN 
                              WHERE ApplicantIncome IS NOT NULL AND LoanAmount IS NOT NULL) AS subquery2)))) AS correlation_coefficient;

WITH income_loan AS (
    SELECT 
        ApplicantIncome,
        LoanAmount
    FROM 
        test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
    WHERE 
        ApplicantIncome IS NOT NULL AND LoanAmount IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome) AS sum_x,
        SUM(LoanAmount) AS sum_y,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome * LoanAmount) AS sum_xy,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome * ApplicantIncome) AS sum_xx,
        SUM(LoanAmount * LoanAmount) AS sum_yy
    FROM 
        income_loan
)
SELECT 
    ROUND((n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / 
           (SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y))), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM 
    stats;

WITH valid_data AS (
    SELECT 
        CAST(ApplicantIncome AS FLOAT) AS ApplicantIncome,
        CAST(LoanAmount AS FLOAT) AS LoanAmount
    FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
    WHERE ApplicantIncome IS NOT NULL 
      AND LoanAmount IS NOT NULL
), 
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome) AS sum_x,
        SUM(LoanAmount) AS sum_y,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome * LoanAmount) AS sum_xy,
        SUM(ApplicantIncome * ApplicantIncome) AS sum_xx,
        SUM(LoanAmount * LoanAmount) AS sum_yy
    FROM valid_data
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(
        (n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / 
        NULLIF(SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y)), 0), 
        2
    ) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM stats;

samples.question: Generate a new feature called "TotalIncome" by adding the ApplicantIncome and CoapplicantIncome columns. Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the TotalIncome column.
samples.constraints: Calculate the mean and standard deviation using the Panda's DataFrame mean() and std() functions distinctively. Round the results to two decimal places.

StatsCTE AS (

samples.question: Explore the distribution of the LoanAmount column based on different values of the Education column. Determine if there is a significant difference in the loan amount between individuals with different educational backgrounds.
samples.constraints: Calculate the mean of LoanAmount for individuals with a 'Graduate' educational background and individuals with a 'Not Graduate' educational background separately. Test if there is a significant difference between these two groups using a t-test with a significance level (alpha) of 0.05. If the p-value is less than 0.05, report there is a significant difference, else report there is no significant difference.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN Education = 'Graduate' THEN LoanAmount END), 2) AS graduate_mean_loan,
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN Education = 'Not Graduate' THEN LoanAmount END), 2) AS not_graduate_mean_loan,
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE Education = 'Graduate') > 1 AND 
             (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE Education = 'Not Graduate') > 1 
        THEN 'significant' 
        ELSE 'no significant difference' 
    END AS significance
FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN;

WITH GraduateLoans AS (
    SELECT ROUND(AVG(LoanAmount), 2) AS graduate_mean_loan
    FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
    WHERE Education = 'Graduate'
),
NotGraduateLoans AS (
    SELECT ROUND(AVG(LoanAmount), 2) AS not_graduate_mean_loan
    FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
    WHERE Education = 'Not Graduate'
),
LoanStats AS (
    SELECT 
        g.graduate_mean_loan,
        n.not_graduate_mean_loan,
        (g.graduate_mean_loan - n.not_graduate_mean_loan) AS difference
    FROM GraduateLoans g, NotGraduateLoans n
)
SELECT 
    graduate_mean_loan,
    not_graduate_mean_loan,
    CASE 
        WHEN ABS(difference) > 0 THEN 'significant' 
        ELSE 'no significant difference' 
    END AS significance
FROM LoanStats;

SELECT 
    ROUND(graduate_mean.loan_amount, 2) AS graduate_mean_loan,
    ROUND(not_graduate_mean.loan_amount, 2) AS not_graduate_mean_loan,
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE Education = 'Graduate') > 1 
             AND (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE Education = 'Not Graduate') > 1 
             THEN 
                 CASE 
                     WHEN (SELECT (AVG(LoanAmount) - AVG(LoanAmount)) / 
                                  SQRT((VARIANCE(LoanAmount) / COUNT(*)) + (VARIANCE(LoanAmount) / COUNT(*))) 
                           FROM (SELECT LoanAmount FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE Education = 'Graduate') AS g, 
                                (SELECT LoanAmount FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE Education = 'Not Graduate') AS ng) < 1.96 
                     THEN 'no significant difference' 
                     ELSE 'significant' 
                 END 
        ELSE 'insufficient data' 
    END AS significance
FROM 
    (SELECT AVG(LoanAmount) AS loan_amount 
     FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN 
     WHERE Education = 'Graduate') AS graduate_mean,
    (SELECT AVG(LoanAmount) AS loan_amount 
     FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN 
     WHERE Education = 'Not Graduate') AS not_graduate_mean;

WITH LoanStats AS (
    SELECT 
        Education,
        ROUND(AVG(LoanAmount), 2) AS mean_loan_amount,
        COUNT(*) AS count_loans,
        SUM(LoanAmount) AS total_loan_amount
    FROM 
        test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
    GROUP BY 
        Education
),
Stats AS (
    SELECT 
        mean_loan_amount,
        count_loans,
        total_loan_amount,
        SUM(total_loan_amount) OVER () AS overall_total,
        SUM(count_loans) OVER () AS overall_count
    FROM 
        LoanStats
)
SELECT 
    MAX(CASE WHEN Education = 'Graduate' THEN mean_loan_amount END) AS graduate_mean_loan,
    MAX(CASE WHEN Education = 'Not Graduate' THEN mean_loan_amount END) AS not_graduate_mean_loan,
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM LoanStats WHERE Education = 'Graduate') > 1 
             AND (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM LoanStats WHERE Education = 'Not Graduate') > 1 
        THEN 
            CASE 
                WHEN ABS((MAX(CASE WHEN Education = 'Graduate' THEN mean_loan_amount END) - 
                            MAX(CASE WHEN Education = 'Not Graduate' THEN mean_loan_amount END)) / 
                            SQRT((SUM(POWER(LoanAmount - mean_loan_amount, 2)) OVER (PARTITION BY Education) / 
                            COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY Education)))) < 1.96 
                THEN 'no significant difference'
                ELSE 'significant difference'
            END
        ELSE 
            'insufficient data for t-test'
    END AS significance
FROM 
    LoanStats;

WITH LoanStats AS (
    SELECT 
        Education,
        ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(LoanAmount, 0)), 2) AS MeanLoanAmount,
        COUNT(*) AS CountLoan
    FROM 
        test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
    WHERE 
        Education IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY 
        Education
),
GraduateStats AS (
    SELECT 
        MeanLoanAmount AS GraduateMeanLoan,
        CountLoan AS GraduateCount
    FROM 
        LoanStats
    WHERE 
        Education = 'Graduate'
),
NotGraduateStats AS (
    SELECT 
        MeanLoanAmount AS NotGraduateMeanLoan,
        CountLoan AS NotGraduateCount
    FROM 
        LoanStats
    WHERE 
        Education = 'Not Graduate'
),
TTest AS (
    SELECT 
        (GraduateMeanLoan - NotGraduateMeanLoan) AS Difference,
        (CAST(GraduateCount AS FLOAT) + CAST(NotGraduateCount AS FLOAT) - 2) AS DegreesOfFreedom,
        (GraduateCount * (GraduateMeanLoan - ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(LoanAmount, 0)), 2)) * (GraduateMeanLoan - ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(LoanAmount, 0)), 2)) 
        + NotGraduateCount * (NotGraduateMeanLoan - ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(LoanAmount, 0)), 2)) * (NotGraduateMeanLoan - ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(LoanAmount, 0)), 2))) / 
        (GraduateCount + NotGraduateCount - 2) AS PooledVariance
    FROM 
        GraduateStats, NotGraduateStats
)
SELECT 
    COALESCE((SELECT GraduateMeanLoan FROM GraduateStats), 0) AS graduate_mean_loan,
    COALESCE((SELECT NotGraduateMeanLoan FROM NotGraduateStats), 0) AS not_graduate_mean_loan,
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT Difference FROM TTest) IS NOT NULL AND (SELECT PooledVariance FROM TTest) IS NOT NULL 
             AND (SELECT (ABS(Difference) / SQRT(PooledVariance * (1.0 / GraduateCount + 1.0 / NotGraduateCount))) < 1.96) THEN 'no significant difference'
        ELSE 'significant' 
    END AS significance
FROM 
    GraduateStats, NotGraduateStats;

samples.question: Perform comprehensive data preprocessing by handling missing values in the Self_Employed and LoanAmount columns. Use different strategies to handle the missing values in each column and compare the impact on the dataset's summary statistics (mean, median, etc.).
samples.constraints: Replace missing values in Self_Employed with 'No' and in LoanAmount with the median value of the column. Calculate the mean, median, and standard deviation of LoanAmount after preprocessing.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(LoanAmount, (SELECT MEDIAN(LoanAmount) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN)), 2) AS mean_loan_amount,
    ROUND((SELECT MEDIAN(COALESCE(LoanAmount, (SELECT MEDIAN(LoanAmount) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN)) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN), 2) AS median_loan_amount,
    ROUND(STDDEV(COALESCE(LoanAmount, (SELECT MEDIAN(LoanAmount) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN))), 2) AS std_dev_loan
FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN;

WITH PreprocessedData AS (
    SELECT 
        Loan_ID,
        Gender,
        Married,
        Dependents,
        Education,
        COALESCE(Self_Employed, 'No') AS Self_Employed,
        ApplicantIncome,
        CoapplicantIncome,
        COALESCE(LoanAmount, (SELECT ROUND(MEDIAN(LoanAmount), 0) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN)) AS LoanAmount,
        Loan_Amount_Term,
        Credit_History,
        Property_Area
    FROM 
        test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
),
SummaryStatistics AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(LoanAmount), 2) AS mean_loan,
        ROUND(MEDIAN(LoanAmount), 2) AS median_loan,
        ROUND(STDDEV(LoanAmount), 2) AS std_dev_loan
    FROM 
        PreprocessedData
)
SELECT 
    std_dev_loan 
FROM 
    SummaryStatistics;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(LoanAmount), 2) AS mean_loan,
    ROUND((SELECT LoanAmount FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE LoanAmount IS NOT NULL ORDER BY LoanAmount LIMIT 1 OFFSET (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE LoanAmount IS NOT NULL) / 2), 2) AS median_loan,
    ROUND(STDDEV(LoanAmount), 2) AS std_dev_loan
FROM (
    SELECT 
        Loan_ID, 
        Gender, 
        Married, 
        Dependents, 
        Education, 
        COALESCE(Self_Employed, 'No') AS Self_Employed, 
        ApplicantIncome, 
        CoapplicantIncome, 
        COALESCE(LoanAmount, (SELECT AVG(LoanAmount) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE LoanAmount IS NOT NULL)) AS LoanAmount, 
        Loan_Amount_Term, 
        Credit_History, 
        Property_Area
    FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
) AS preprocessed_data;

WITH PreprocessedData AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        COALESCE(Self_Employed, 'No') AS Self_Employed_Filled,
        COALESCE(LoanAmount, (SELECT median(LoanAmount) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN)) AS LoanAmount_Filled
    FROM 
        test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
),
LoanAmountStats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(LoanAmount_Filled), 2) AS mean_loan,
        ROUND(median(LoanAmount_Filled), 2) AS median_loan,
        ROUND(STDEV(LoanAmount_Filled), 2) AS std_dev_loan
    FROM 
        PreprocessedData
)
SELECT std_dev_loan FROM LoanAmountStats;

WITH PreprocessedData AS (
    SELECT 
        Loan_ID,
        Gender,
        Married,
        Dependents,
        Education,
        COALESCE(Self_Employed, 'No') AS Self_Employed,
        ApplicantIncome,
        CoapplicantIncome,
        COALESCE(LoanAmount, (SELECT ROUND(median(LoanAmount), 2) FROM test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN WHERE LoanAmount IS NOT NULL)) AS LoanAmount,
        Loan_Amount_Term,
        Credit_History,
        Property_Area
    FROM 
        test_Y3wMUE5_7gLdaTN
    WHERE 
        Self_Employed IS NOT NULL OR LoanAmount IS NOT NULL
),
SummaryStats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(LoanAmount), 2) AS mean_loan,
        ROUND(median(LoanAmount), 2) AS median_loan,
        ROUND(STDEV(LoanAmount), 2) AS std_dev_loan
    FROM 
        PreprocessedData
    WHERE 
        LoanAmount IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    std_dev_loan 
FROM 
    SummaryStats;

samples.question: Which country has the highest happiness score?
samples.constraints: Find the country with the highest happiness score in the dataset. If two or more countries have the same highest happiness score, return all of them.

variants.sql: SELECT Country FROM 2015 WHERE Happiness Score = (SELECT MAX(Happiness Score) FROM 2015);

WITH MaxScore AS (
    SELECT MAX(Happiness_Score) AS Highest_Score
    FROM 2015
),
CountriesWithMaxScore AS (
    SELECT Country
    FROM 2015
