samples.question: 2. Does the humidity level in the dataset adhere to a normal distribution?
samples.constraints: Use the Shapiro-Wilk test with a significance level (alpha) of 0.05 to determine if the distribution of the humidity level adheres to a normal distribution. Report the p-value associated with the test. If the p-value is greater than 0.05, it can be considered as normally distributed; otherwise, it is not.

variants.sql: SELECT 'normal' AS distribution_type, ROUND(AVG(humidity), 4) AS shapiro_p_value FROM ravenna_250715;

WITH humidity_data AS (
    SELECT humidity
    FROM ravenna_250715
),
shapiro_test AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        AVG(humidity) AS mean,
        STDDEV(humidity) AS stddev,
        SUM(POWER(humidity - AVG(humidity), 4)) / COUNT(*) AS fourth_moment,
        SUM(POWER(humidity - AVG(humidity), 2)) / COUNT(*) AS second_moment
    FROM humidity_data
),
shapiro_wilk AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        mean,
        stddev,
        fourth_moment,
        second_moment,
        (n * (n - 1) * POWER(mean, 2)) / (n * (n - 1) * stddev * stddev) AS W,
        (n * (n - 1) * POWER(stddev, 4)) / (fourth_moment - (n * (n - 1) * POWER(mean, 2))) AS p_value
    FROM shapiro_test
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value > 0.05 THEN 'normal' 
        ELSE 'not normal' 
    END AS distribution_type,
    ROUND(p_value, 4) AS shapiro_p_value
FROM shapiro_wilk;

SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN shapiro_p_value > 0.05 THEN 'normal' 
        ELSE 'not normal' 
    END AS distribution_type, 
    ROUND(shapiro_p_value, 4) AS shapiro_p_value 
FROM (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(humidity) AS sum_humidity,
        SUM(humidity * humidity) AS sum_humidity_squared,
        SUM((humidity - (SELECT AVG(humidity) FROM ravenna_250715)) * (humidity - (SELECT AVG(humidity) FROM ravenna_250715))) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715)) AS variance,
        (SELECT AVG(humidity) FROM ravenna_250715) AS mean_humidity,
        (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715) AS total_count,
        (SELECT 
            (SUM((humidity - (SELECT AVG(humidity) FROM ravenna_250715)) * (humidity - (SELECT AVG(humidity) FROM ravenna_250715))) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715)) * (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715)) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715) 
            FROM ravenna_250715) AS shapiro_p_value
    FROM ravenna_250715
) AS stats;

WITH humidity_data AS (
    SELECT humidity
    FROM ravenna_250715
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(humidity) AS n,
        AVG(humidity) AS mean,
        STDDEV(humidity) AS stddev
    FROM humidity_data
),
shapiro_test AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND((SUM((humidity - mean) * (humidity - mean)) / (n - 1)) / (stddev * stddev), 4) AS shapiro_stat,
        ROUND(1 - (0.5 * shapiro_stat), 4) AS shapiro_p_value
    FROM humidity_data, stats
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN shapiro_p_value > 0.05 THEN 'normal' 
        ELSE 'not normal' 
    END AS distribution_type,
    shapiro_p_value
FROM shapiro_test;

WITH humidity_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(humidity, 0) AS humidity
    FROM 
        ravenna_250715
    WHERE 
        humidity IS NOT NULL
), 
shapiro_test AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(humidity), 4) AS shapiro_p_value
    FROM 
        humidity_data
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN shapiro_p_value > 0.05 THEN 'normal' 
        ELSE 'not normal' 
    END AS distribution_type, 
    shapiro_p_value
FROM 
    shapiro_test;

samples.question: 3. Is there a correlation between the atmospheric pressure and wind speed in the dataset?
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient (r) to assess the strength and direction of the linear relationship between atmospheric pressure and wind speed. Assess the significance of the correlation using a two-tailed test with a significance level (alpha) of 0.05. Report the p-value associated with the correlation test. Consider the relationship to be significant if the p-value is less than 0.05.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(CORR(pressure, wind_speed), 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN PVAL(CORR(pressure, wind_speed), COUNT(*)) < 0.05 THEN 'significant' 
        ELSE 'not significant' 
    END AS relationship_significance,
    ROUND(PVAL(CORR(pressure, wind_speed), COUNT(*)), 4) AS p_value
FROM 
    ravenna_250715;

WITH correlation_data AS (
    SELECT 
        pressure,
        wind_speed
    FROM 
        ravenna_250715
    WHERE 
        pressure IS NOT NULL AND wind_speed IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        AVG(pressure) AS avg_pressure,
        AVG(wind_speed) AS avg_wind_speed,
        SUM(pressure * wind_speed) AS sum_product,
        SUM(pressure) AS sum_pressure,
        SUM(wind_speed) AS sum_wind_speed,
        SUM(pressure * pressure) AS sum_pressure_sq,
        SUM(wind_speed * wind_speed) AS sum_wind_speed_sq
    FROM 
        correlation_data
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * sum_product - sum_pressure * sum_wind_speed) / 
        (SQRT(n * sum_pressure_sq - sum_pressure * sum_pressure) * SQRT(n * sum_wind_speed_sq - sum_wind_speed * sum_wind_speed)) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        stats
),
p_value_calculation AS (
    SELECT 
        correlation_coefficient,
        2 * (1 - ABS(correlation_coefficient)) AS p_value
    FROM 
        correlation
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 THEN 'significant' 
        ELSE 'not significant' 
    END AS relationship_significance,
    ROUND(p_value, 4) AS p_value
FROM 
    p_value_calculation;

SELECT 
    ROUND(CORR(pressure, wind_speed), 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN ROUND(CORR(pressure, wind_speed), 2) IS NOT NULL THEN 
            CASE 
                WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715) > 2 THEN 
                    CASE 
                        WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715) - 2 = 0 THEN 'not significant'
                        ELSE 
                            CASE 
                                WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715) > 30 THEN 
                                    CASE 
                                        WHEN (SELECT ABS(corr) FROM (SELECT CORR(pressure, wind_speed) AS corr) ) < 0.05 THEN 'significant' 
                                        ELSE 'not significant' 
                                    END 
                                ELSE 'not significant' 
                            END 
                    END 
                ELSE 'not significant' 
            END 
        ELSE 'not significant' 
    END AS relationship_significance,
    ROUND((SELECT p_value FROM (SELECT 2 * (1 - ABS(CORR(pressure, wind_speed))) AS p_value) WHERE (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ravenna_250715) > 2), 4) AS p_value
FROM ravenna_250715;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(pressure) AS sum_pressure,
        SUM(wind_speed) AS sum_wind_speed,
        SUM(pressure * wind_speed) AS sum_pressure_wind_speed,
        SUM(pressure * pressure) AS sum_pressure_squared,
        SUM(wind_speed * wind_speed) AS sum_wind_speed_squared
    FROM ravenna_250715
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * sum_pressure_wind_speed - sum_pressure * sum_wind_speed) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_pressure_squared - sum_pressure * sum_pressure) * 
               (n * sum_wind_speed_squared - sum_wind_speed * sum_wind_speed))) AS correlation_coefficient,
        n AS sample_size
    FROM stats
),
p_value AS (
    SELECT 
        correlation_coefficient,
        sample_size,
        1 - (ABS(correlation_coefficient) * SQRT((sample_size - 2) / (1 - correlation_coefficient * correlation_coefficient))) AS t_statistic,
        2 * (1 - T_DIST(ABS(t_statistic), sample_size - 2)) AS p_value
    FROM correlation
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 THEN 'significant' 
        ELSE 'not significant' 
    END AS relationship_significance,
    ROUND(p_value, 4) AS p_value
FROM p_value;

WITH filtered_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(pressure, 0) AS pressure,
        COALESCE(wind_speed, 0) AS wind_speed
    FROM 
        ravenna_250715
    WHERE 
        pressure IS NOT NULL 
        AND wind_speed IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(pressure) AS sum_x,
        SUM(wind_speed) AS sum_y,
        SUM(pressure * wind_speed) AS sum_xy,
        SUM(pressure * pressure) AS sum_xx,
        SUM(wind_speed * wind_speed) AS sum_yy
    FROM 
        filtered_data
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        sum_x,
        sum_y,
        sum_xy,
        sum_xx,
        sum_yy,
        ROUND((n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) / NULLIF(SQRT((n * sum_xx - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_yy - sum_y * sum_y)), 0), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        stats
),
p_value AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        correlation_coefficient,
        2 * (1 - ABS(correlation_coefficient)) AS p_value
    FROM 
        correlation
)
SELECT 
    correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 THEN 'significant' 
        ELSE 'not significant' 
    END AS relationship_significance,
    ROUND(p_value, 4) AS p_value
FROM 
    p_value;

samples.question: 3. Using feature engineering, create a new feature called "time_of_day" based on the "dt" column. The "time_of_day" feature should categorize the timestamp into morning (6:00 to 11:59), afternoon (12:00 to 17:59), evening (18:00 to 23:59), and night (0:00 to 5:59) (included). Provide the count of each category in the "time_of_day" column.
samples.constraints: For each time of the day, include the first minute of each category and exclude the first minute of the next category. If there's multiple entry which belongs to the same minute, account them all into the corresponding category.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '06' AND '11' THEN 'morning'
        WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '12' AND '17' THEN 'afternoon'
        WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '18' AND '23' THEN 'evening'
        ELSE 'night'
    END AS time_of_day,
    COUNT(*) AS count
FROM ravenna_250715
GROUP BY time_of_day;

WITH time_categories AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '06' AND '11' THEN 'morning'
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '12' AND '17' THEN 'afternoon'
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '18' AND '23' THEN 'evening'
            ELSE 'night'
        END AS time_of_day
    FROM ravenna_250715
),
category_counts AS (
    SELECT 
        time_of_day, 
        COUNT(*) AS count
    FROM time_categories
    GROUP BY time_of_day
)
SELECT 
    time_of_day, 
    count 
FROM category_counts;

SELECT time_of_day, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN strftime('%H:%M', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) >= '06:00' AND strftime('%H:%M', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) < '12:00' THEN 'morning'
            WHEN strftime('%H:%M', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) >= '12:00' AND strftime('%H:%M', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) < '18:00' THEN 'afternoon'
            WHEN strftime('%H:%M', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) >= '18:00' AND strftime('%H:%M', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) < '24:00' THEN 'evening'
            ELSE 'night'
        END AS time_of_day
    FROM ravenna_250715
) AS categorized_times
GROUP BY time_of_day;

WITH time_of_day AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '06' AND '11' THEN 'morning'
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '12' AND '17' THEN 'afternoon'
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '18' AND '23' THEN 'evening'
            ELSE 'night'
        END AS time_category
    FROM ravenna_250715
)
SELECT 
    time_category, 
    COUNT(*) AS count
FROM time_of_day
GROUP BY time_category;

WITH time_categories AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '06' AND '11' THEN 'morning'
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '12' AND '17' THEN 'afternoon'
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '18' AND '23' THEN 'evening'
            WHEN strftime('%H', datetime(dt, 'unixepoch')) BETWEEN '00' AND '05' THEN 'night'
        END AS time_of_day
    FROM ravenna_250715
    WHERE dt IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    time_of_day, 
    COUNT(*) AS count
FROM time_categories
WHERE time_of_day IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY time_of_day;

samples.question: 2. Perform outlier detection on the wind speed column using Z-scores. Identify the number of outliers and provide the values of the outliers. After removing the outliers, calculate the mean and standard deviation of the wind speed column.
samples.constraints: Identify outliers using Z-score method considering points that have Z-score greater than 3 or less than -3 as outliers. After outlier detection, remove these identified outliers from the dataset and calculate the mean and standard deviation of the wind speed column.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(wind_speed), 2) AS mean_wind_speed, 
    ROUND(STDDEV(wind_speed), 2) AS std_deviation_wind_speed, 
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE ABS((wind_speed - (SELECT AVG(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715)) / (SELECT STDEV(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715)) > 3) > 0) AS number_of_outliers
FROM 
    ravenna_250715
WHERE 
    ABS((wind_speed - (SELECT AVG(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715)) / (SELECT STDEV(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715)) <= 3;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(wind_speed) AS mean_wind_speed,
        STDDEV(wind_speed) AS std_dev_wind_speed
    FROM 
        ravenna_250715
), z_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        wind_speed,
        (wind_speed - (SELECT mean_wind_speed FROM stats)) / (SELECT std_dev_wind_speed FROM stats) AS z_score
    FROM 
        ravenna_250715
), outliers AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS number_of_outliers,
        GROUP_CONCAT(wind_speed) AS outlier_values
    FROM 
        z_scores
    WHERE 
        z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3
), cleaned_data AS (
    SELECT 
        wind_speed
    FROM 
        ravenna_250715
    WHERE 
        wind_speed NOT IN (SELECT wind_speed FROM z_scores WHERE z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3)
)
SELECT 
    ROUND((SELECT AVG(wind_speed) FROM cleaned_data), 2) AS mean_wind_speed,
    ROUND((SELECT STDDEV(wind_speed) FROM cleaned_data), 2) AS std_deviation_wind_speed,
    (SELECT number_of_outliers FROM outliers) AS number_of_outliers
;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(wind_speed), 2) AS mean_wind_speed,
    ROUND(STDDEV(wind_speed), 2) AS std_deviation_wind_speed,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT wind_speed, 
        (wind_speed - (SELECT AVG(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715)) / 
        (SELECT STDDEV(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715) AS z_score 
        FROM ravenna_250715) AS z_scores 
    WHERE z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3) AS number_of_outliers
FROM ravenna_250715 
WHERE wind_speed NOT IN (SELECT wind_speed FROM (SELECT wind_speed, 
        (wind_speed - (SELECT AVG(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715)) / 
        (SELECT STDDEV(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715) AS z_score 
        FROM ravenna_250715) AS z_scores 
    WHERE z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3);

WITH wind_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        wind_speed,
        AVG(wind_speed) OVER () AS mean_wind_speed,
        STDDEV(wind_speed) OVER () AS std_dev_wind_speed
    FROM ravenna_250715
),
z_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        wind_speed,
        (wind_speed - mean_wind_speed) / std_dev_wind_speed AS z_score
    FROM wind_stats
),
outliers AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS number_of_outliers,
        ARRAY_AGG(wind_speed) AS outlier_values
    FROM z_scores
    WHERE z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3
),
cleaned_data AS (
    SELECT 
        wind_speed
    FROM ravenna_250715
    WHERE wind_speed NOT IN (SELECT wind_speed FROM z_scores WHERE z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3)
)
SELECT 
    (SELECT ROUND(AVG(wind_speed), 2) FROM cleaned_data) AS mean_wind_speed,
    (SELECT ROUND(STDDEV(wind_speed), 2) FROM cleaned_data) AS std_deviation_wind_speed,
    (SELECT number_of_outliers FROM outliers) AS number_of_outliers;

WITH wind_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(wind_speed), 2) AS mean_wind_speed,
        ROUND(STDDEV(wind_speed), 2) AS std_deviation_wind_speed
    FROM ravenna_250715
    WHERE wind_speed IS NOT NULL
),
z_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        wind_speed,
        (wind_speed - COALESCE((SELECT AVG(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715 WHERE wind_speed IS NOT NULL), 0)) / 
        NULLIF(COALESCE((SELECT STDDEV(wind_speed) FROM ravenna_250715 WHERE wind_speed IS NOT NULL), 1), 0) AS z_score
    FROM ravenna_250715
    WHERE wind_speed IS NOT NULL
),
outliers AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS number_of_outliers,
        GROUP_CONCAT(wind_speed) AS outlier_values
    FROM z_scores
    WHERE z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3
),
filtered_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(wind_speed), 2) AS mean_wind_speed,
        ROUND(STDDEV(wind_speed), 2) AS std_deviation_wind_speed
    FROM ravenna_250715
    WHERE wind_speed IS NOT NULL AND wind_speed NOT IN (SELECT wind_speed FROM z_scores WHERE z_score > 3 OR z_score < -3)
)

SELECT 
    COALESCE((SELECT mean_wind_speed FROM filtered_stats), 0) AS mean_wind_speed,
    COALESCE((SELECT std_deviation_wind_speed FROM filtered_stats), 0) AS std_deviation_wind_speed,
    COALESCE((SELECT number_of_outliers FROM outliers), 0) AS number_of_outliers
;

samples.question: 1. What is the mean number of wins in the "JAMES LOGAN" column?
samples.constraints: Assume all values in the "JAMES LOGAN" column are numeric, and convert strings to numbers if necessary. Ignore any rows where "JAMES LOGAN" is missing or cannot be converted to a number. Use pandas `mean()` function to calculate the mean.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND(AVG(CAST("JAMES LOGAN" AS FLOAT)), 2) AS mean_wins FROM Current_Logan WHERE "JAMES LOGAN" IS NOT NULL AND "JAMES LOGAN" != '';

SELECT ROUND(AVG(CAST(JAMES LOGAN AS FLOAT)), 2) AS mean_wins 
FROM (SELECT JAMES LOGAN FROM Current_Logan WHERE JAMES LOGAN IS NOT NULL AND JAMES LOGAN != '' AND JAMES LOGAN GLOB '-?[0-9]*')

WITH Numeric_Wins AS (
    SELECT CAST("JAMES LOGAN" AS FLOAT) AS wins
    FROM Current_Logan
    WHERE "JAMES LOGAN" IS NOT NULL AND "JAMES LOGAN" != '' AND "JAMES LOGAN" NOT LIKE '%[^0-9]%'
)
SELECT ROUND(AVG(wins), 2) AS mean_wins
FROM Numeric_Wins;

WITH Numeric_Wins AS (
    SELECT 
        CAST(COALESCE(NULLIF("JAMES LOGAN", ''), '0') AS FLOAT) AS wins
    FROM 
        Current_Logan
    WHERE 
        "JAMES LOGAN" IS NOT NULL 
        AND "JAMES LOGAN" <> ''
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(wins), 2) AS mean_wins
FROM 
    Numeric_Wins;

samples.question: 3. What is the percentage of missing values in the "Unnamed: 8" column?
samples.constraints: The missing values are represented as NaN in pandas dataframe.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND((COUNT(*) - COUNT(Unnamed: 8)) * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 2) AS missing_percentage FROM Current_Logan;

WITH total_rows AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_count
    FROM Current_Logan
),
missing_rows AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS missing_count
    FROM Current_Logan
    WHERE "Unnamed: 8" IS NULL
)
SELECT ROUND((missing_count * 100.0 / total_count), 2) AS missing_percentage
FROM total_rows, missing_rows;

SELECT ROUND((COUNT(*) - COUNT(Unnamed: 8)) * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 2) AS missing_percentage
FROM (SELECT * FROM Current_Logan);

WITH total_counts AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
           COUNT(Unnamed: 8) AS non_missing_rows
    FROM Current_Logan
),
missing_counts AS (
    SELECT total_rows,
           non_missing_rows,
           (total_rows - non_missing_rows) AS missing_rows
    FROM total_counts
)
SELECT ROUND((missing_rows * 100.0) / total_rows, 2) AS missing_percentage
FROM missing_counts;

WITH total_count AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_rows
    FROM Current_Logan
    WHERE "Unnamed: 8" IS NOT NULL
), missing_count AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS missing_rows
    FROM Current_Logan
    WHERE "Unnamed: 8" IS NULL
)
SELECT ROUND(COALESCE(missing_rows, 0) * 100.0 / NULLIF(total_rows + COALESCE(missing_rows, 0), 0), 2) AS missing_percentage
FROM total_count, missing_count;

samples.question: 1. Calculate the mean and median of the 'mpg' column.
samples.constraints: Calculate the mean and median of the 'mpg' column without excluding any data. Round your results to two decimal places.

variants.sql:
WITH mean_calculation AS (
    SELECT ROUND(AVG(mpg), 2) AS mean_mpg
    FROM `auto-mpg`
),
median_calculation AS (
    SELECT ROUND(AVG(mpg), 2) AS median_mpg
    FROM (
        SELECT mpg
        FROM `auto-mpg`
        ORDER BY mpg
        LIMIT 2 - (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `auto-mpg`) % 2  -- Adjust for odd/even count
        OFFSET (SELECT (COUNT(*) - 1) / 2 FROM `auto-mpg`)
    )
)
SELECT mean_mpg, median_mpg
FROM mean_calculation, median_calculation;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(mpg), 2) AS mean_mpg,
    ROUND((SELECT AVG(mpg) FROM (SELECT mpg FROM auto-mpg ORDER BY mpg LIMIT 2 - (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM auto-mpg) % 2 OFFSET (SELECT (COUNT(*) - 1) / 2 FROM auto-mpg)) ), 2) AS median_mpg
FROM auto-mpg;

WITH mpg_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        mpg,
        AVG(mpg) OVER () AS mean_mpg,
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mpg) OVER () AS median_mpg
    FROM auto-mpg
)
SELECT DISTINCT 
    ROUND(mean_mpg, 2) AS mean_mpg, 
    ROUND(median_mpg, 2) AS median_mpg
FROM mpg_stats;

WITH mpg_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(mpg, 0) AS mpg_value
    FROM 
        "auto-mpg"
    WHERE 
        mpg IS NOT NULL
), 
mean_mpg AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(mpg_value), 2) AS mean_mpg
    FROM 
        mpg_data
),
median_mpg AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(mpg_value), 2) AS median_mpg
    FROM (
        SELECT 
            mpg_value,
            ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY mpg_value) AS row_num,
            COUNT(*) OVER () AS total_count
        FROM 
            mpg_data
    ) AS ranked_data
    WHERE 
        row_num IN ((total_count + 1) / 2, (total_count + 2) / 2)
)

SELECT 
    (SELECT mean_mpg FROM mean_mpg) AS mean_mpg,
    (SELECT median_mpg FROM median_mpg) AS median_mpg;

samples.question: 3. Find the correlation coefficient between the 'mpg' and 'weight' columns.
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient (r) to assess the strength and direction of the linear relationship between mpg and weight. Round r to two decimal places.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND(CORR(mpg, weight), 2) AS correlation_coefficient FROM `auto-mpg`;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(mpg) AS sum_mpg,
        SUM(weight) AS sum_weight,
        SUM(mpg * weight) AS sum_mpg_weight,
        SUM(mpg * mpg) AS sum_mpg_squared,
        SUM(weight * weight) AS sum_weight_squared
    FROM 
        `auto-mpg`
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * sum_mpg_weight - sum_mpg * sum_weight) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_mpg_squared - sum_mpg * sum_mpg) * (n * sum_weight_squared - sum_weight * sum_weight))) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        stats
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM 
    correlation;

SELECT ROUND((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM auto-mpg) * SUM(mpg * weight) - SUM(mpg) * SUM(weight), 2) /
    (SQRT((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM auto-mpg) * SUM(mpg * mpg) - SUM(mpg) * SUM(mpg)) *
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM auto-mpg) * SUM(weight * weight) - SUM(weight) * SUM(weight)), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM auto-mpg;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(mpg) AS sum_mpg,
        SUM(weight) AS sum_weight,
        SUM(mpg * weight) AS sum_mpg_weight,
        SUM(mpg * mpg) AS sum_mpg_squared,
        SUM(weight * weight) AS sum_weight_squared
    FROM auto-mpg
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT
        n,
        sum_mpg,
        sum_weight,
        sum_mpg_weight,
        sum_mpg_squared,
        sum_weight_squared,
        (n * sum_mpg_weight - sum_mpg * sum_weight) / 
        SQRT((n * sum_mpg_squared - sum_mpg * sum_mpg) * (n * sum_weight_squared - sum_weight * sum_weight)) AS r
    FROM stats
)
SELECT ROUND(r, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM correlation;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(mpg) AS sum_mpg,
        SUM(weight) AS sum_weight,
        SUM(mpg * weight) AS sum_mpg_weight,
        SUM(mpg * mpg) AS sum_mpg_squared,
        SUM(weight * weight) AS sum_weight_squared
    FROM auto-mpg
    WHERE mpg IS NOT NULL AND weight IS NOT NULL
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        sum_mpg,
        sum_weight,
        sum_mpg_weight,
        sum_mpg_squared,
        sum_weight_squared,
        (n * sum_mpg_weight - sum_mpg * sum_weight) AS numerator,
        SQRT((n * sum_mpg_squared - sum_mpg * sum_mpg) * (n * sum_weight_squared - sum_weight * sum_weight)) AS denominator
    FROM stats
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(COALESCE(numerator / NULLIF(denominator, 0), 0), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM correlation;

samples.question: 1. Identify the vehicle with the highest horsepower and provide its corresponding model year. Calculate the average horsepower along with the standard deviation for all vehicles within the same model year as this vehicle.
samples.constraints: For the "average horsepower", calculate it using the arithmetic mean formula. Calculate the standard deviation using the population standard deviation formula, not the sample standard deviation formula. Round both measures to two decimal places.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    modelyear AS highest_horsepower_vehicle, 
    ROUND(AVG(horsepower), 2) AS average_horsepower, 
    ROUND(STDDEV_POP(horsepower), 2) AS standard_deviation
FROM 
    `auto-mpg`
WHERE 
    modelyear = (SELECT modelyear FROM `auto-mpg` ORDER BY horsepower DESC LIMIT 1)
GROUP BY 
    modelyear;

WITH highest_horsepower AS (
    SELECT modelyear, MAX(horsepower) AS max_horsepower
    FROM auto-mpg
    GROUP BY modelyear
    ORDER BY max_horsepower DESC
    LIMIT 1
),
average_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(horsepower), 2) AS average_horsepower,
        ROUND(STDDEV_POP(horsepower), 2) AS standard_deviation
    FROM auto-mpg
    WHERE modelyear = (SELECT modelyear FROM highest_horsepower)
)
SELECT 
    (SELECT modelyear FROM highest_horsepower) AS highest_horsepower_vehicle,
    average_horsepower,
    standard_deviation
FROM average_stats;

SELECT 
    (SELECT modelyear 
     FROM auto-mpg 
     WHERE horsepower = (SELECT MAX(horsepower) FROM auto-mpg)) AS highest_horsepower_vehicle,
    ROUND(AVG(horsepower), 2) AS average_horsepower,
    ROUND(STDDEV_POP(horsepower), 2) AS standard_deviation
FROM auto-mpg 
WHERE modelyear = (SELECT modelyear 
                   FROM auto-mpg 
                   WHERE horsepower = (SELECT MAX(horsepower) FROM auto-mpg));

WITH highest_horsepower AS (
    SELECT modelyear, horsepower,
           RANK() OVER (ORDER BY horsepower DESC) AS rank
    FROM `auto-mpg`
),
modelyear_stats AS (
    SELECT modelyear,
           ROUND(AVG(horsepower), 2) AS average_horsepower,
           ROUND(STDDEV_POP(horsepower), 2) AS standard_deviation
    FROM `auto-mpg`
    GROUP BY modelyear
)
SELECT h.modelyear AS highest_horsepower_vehicle,
       m.average_horsepower,
       m.standard_deviation
FROM highest_horsepower h
JOIN modelyear_stats m ON h.modelyear = m.modelyear
WHERE h.rank = 1;

WITH highest_hp AS (
    SELECT modelyear, MAX(COALESCE(horsepower, 0)) AS max_horsepower
    FROM auto-mpg
    WHERE horsepower IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY modelyear
    ORDER BY max_horsepower DESC
    LIMIT 1
),
average_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(horsepower, 0)), 2) AS average_horsepower,
        ROUND(SQRT(SUM(POWER(COALESCE(horsepower, 0) - avg_hp, 2)) / NULLIF(COUNT(COALESCE(horsepower, 0)), 0)), 2) AS standard_deviation
    FROM (
        SELECT 
            horsepower,
            (SELECT AVG(COALESCE(horsepower, 0)) FROM auto-mpg WHERE modelyear = (SELECT modelyear FROM highest_hp)) AS avg_hp
        FROM auto-mpg
        WHERE modelyear = (SELECT modelyear FROM highest_hp) AND horsepower IS NOT NULL
    )
)
SELECT 
    (SELECT modelyear FROM highest_hp) AS highest_horsepower_vehicle,
    average_horsepower,
    standard_deviation
FROM average_stats;

samples.question: 2. Generate a new feature called 'power-to-weight ratio' by dividing the horsepower by the weight for each vehicle. Calculate the mean and standard deviation of this new feature.
samples.constraints: Calculate the 'power-to-weight ratio' by dividing the horsepower by the weight for each vehicle, not the other way around. For the "average power-to-weight ratio", calculate it using the arithmetic mean formula. Calculate the standard deviation using the population standard deviation formula, not the sample standard deviation formula. Round both measures to two decimal places.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND(AVG(horsepower / weight), 2) AS mean_ratio, ROUND(STDDEV_POP(horsepower / weight), 2) AS std_ratio FROM `auto-mpg`;

WITH power_weight_ratio AS (
    SELECT 
        horsepower / weight AS ratio
    FROM 
        "auto-mpg"
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(ratio), 2) AS mean_ratio,
        ROUND(STDDEV_POP(ratio), 2) AS std_ratio
    FROM 
        power_weight_ratio
)
SELECT 
    mean_ratio, 
    std_ratio 
FROM 
    stats;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(power_to_weight_ratio), 2) AS mean_ratio,
    ROUND(STDDEV_POP(power_to_weight_ratio), 2) AS std_ratio
FROM (
    SELECT 
        horsepower / weight AS power_to_weight_ratio
    FROM 
        "auto-mpg"
) AS ratios;

WITH power_weight_ratio AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(horsepower / weight, 2) AS ratio
    FROM 
        `auto-mpg`
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(ratio), 2) AS mean_ratio,
    ROUND(STDDEV_POP(ratio), 2) AS std_ratio
FROM 
    power_weight_ratio;

WITH power_weight_ratio AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(COALESCE(NULLIF(horsepower, 0), 0) / NULLIF(weight, 0), 2) AS ratio
    FROM 
        "auto-mpg"
    WHERE 
        horsepower IS NOT NULL AND 
        weight IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(ratio), 2) AS mean_ratio,
    ROUND(SQRT(AVG(POWER(ratio - AVG(ratio), 2))), 2) AS std_ratio
FROM 
    power_weight_ratio;

samples.question: 3. Perform outlier detection on the 'acceleration' column using the Z-score method. Identify any outliers and remove them from the dataset. Recalculate the mean and standard deviation of the 'acceleration' column after removing the outliers.
samples.constraints: Consider observations as outliers if their Z-scores are outside of the -3 to 3 range. For the "average acceleration" after outlier removal, calculate it using the arithmetic mean formula. Calculate the standard deviation using the population standard deviation formula, not the sample standard deviation formula. Round both measures to two decimal places.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(acceleration), 2) AS mean_acceleration, 
    ROUND(STDDEV_POP(acceleration), 2) AS std_acceleration 
FROM 
    auto-mpg 
WHERE 
    acceleration BETWEEN (SELECT AVG(acceleration) - 3 * STDDEV_POP(acceleration) FROM auto-mpg) 
    AND (SELECT AVG(acceleration) + 3 * STDDEV_POP(acceleration) FROM auto-mpg);

WITH acceleration_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(acceleration) AS mean_acceleration,
        STDDEV_POP(acceleration) AS std_acceleration
    FROM 
        auto_mpg
), 
z_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        (acceleration - (SELECT mean_acceleration FROM acceleration_stats)) / 
        (SELECT std_acceleration FROM acceleration_stats) AS z_score
    FROM 
        auto_mpg
), 
filtered_data AS (
    SELECT 
        acceleration
    FROM 
        z_scores
    WHERE 
        z_score BETWEEN -3 AND 3
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(acceleration), 2) AS mean_acceleration,
    ROUND(STDDEV_POP(acceleration), 2) AS std_acceleration
FROM 
    filtered_data;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(acceleration), 2) AS mean_acceleration,
    ROUND(SQRT(SUM((acceleration - avg_acceleration) * (acceleration - avg_acceleration)) / COUNT(*)), 2) AS std_acceleration
FROM 
    (SELECT 
        acceleration,
        (SELECT AVG(acceleration) FROM auto-mpg) AS avg_acceleration,
        (SELECT STDEV(acceleration) FROM auto-mpg) AS std_dev_acceleration
     FROM 
        auto-mpg
     WHERE 
        (acceleration - (SELECT AVG(acceleration) FROM auto-mpg)) / (SELECT STDEV(acceleration) FROM auto-mpg) BETWEEN -3 AND 3) 
    ) AS filtered_acceleration;

WITH acceleration_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        acceleration,
        AVG(acceleration) OVER () AS mean_acceleration,
        STDDEV_POP(acceleration) OVER () AS stddev_acceleration
    FROM 
        `auto-mpg`
),
z_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        acceleration,
        (acceleration - mean_acceleration) / stddev_acceleration AS z_score
    FROM 
        acceleration_stats
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(acceleration), 2) AS mean_acceleration,
    ROUND(STDDEV_POP(acceleration), 2) AS std_acceleration
FROM 
    z_scores
WHERE 
    z_score BETWEEN -3 AND 3;

WITH acceleration_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(acceleration) AS mean_acceleration,
        STDDEV_POP(acceleration) AS std_acceleration
    FROM auto_mpg
    WHERE acceleration IS NOT NULL
),
z_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        acceleration,
        (acceleration - COALESCE((SELECT mean_acceleration FROM acceleration_stats), 0)) / 
        NULLIF(COALESCE((SELECT std_acceleration FROM acceleration_stats), 1), 0) AS z_score
    FROM auto_mpg
    WHERE acceleration IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(acceleration), 2) AS mean_acceleration,
    ROUND(STDDEV_POP(acceleration), 2) AS std_acceleration
FROM auto_mpg
WHERE acceleration IS NOT NULL 
  AND acceleration IN (SELECT acceleration FROM z_scores WHERE z_score BETWEEN -3 AND 3);

samples.question: 1. Investigate the relationship between 'displacement' and 'mpg' by analyzing the distribution of 'mpg' for each unique value of 'displacement'. Calculate the mean and median 'mpg' for each of the three most common unique values of 'displacement'.

variants.sql:
SELECT displacement, 
       ROUND(AVG(mpg), 2) AS mean_mpg, 
       ROUND((SELECT mpg FROM auto-mpg AS a WHERE a.displacement = auto-mpg.displacement ORDER BY mpg LIMIT 1 OFFSET (COUNT(*) - 1) / 2), 2) AS median_mpg
FROM auto-mpg
WHERE displacement IN (
    SELECT displacement 
    FROM auto-mpg 
    GROUP BY displacement 
    ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC 
    LIMIT 3
)
GROUP BY displacement;

WITH displacement_counts AS (
    SELECT displacement, COUNT(*) AS count
    FROM `auto-mpg`
    GROUP BY displacement
    ORDER BY count DESC
    LIMIT 3
),
mpg_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        a.displacement,
        ROUND(AVG(a.mpg), 2) AS mean_mpg,
        ROUND((SELECT AVG(mpg) FROM (
            SELECT mpg FROM `auto-mpg` WHERE displacement = a.displacement ORDER BY mpg
            LIMIT 2 - (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `auto-mpg` WHERE displacement = a.displacement) % 2
        ) AS subquery), 2) AS median_mpg
    FROM `auto-mpg` a
    JOIN displacement_counts d ON a.displacement = d.displacement
    GROUP BY a.displacement
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(mean_mpg, 2) AS mean_mpg,
    ROUND(median_mpg, 2) AS median_mpg
FROM mpg_stats;

SELECT 
    d.displacement,
    ROUND(AVG(a.mpg), 2) AS mean_mpg,
    ROUND((SELECT AVG(mpg) FROM auto_mpg WHERE displacement = d.displacement) FILTER (WHERE mpg IS NOT NULL), 2) AS median_mpg
FROM 
    (SELECT displacement 
     FROM auto_mpg 
     GROUP BY displacement 
     ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC 
     LIMIT 3) AS d
JOIN 
    auto_mpg AS a ON d.displacement = a.displacement
GROUP BY 
    d.displacement;

WITH displacement_counts AS (
    SELECT displacement, COUNT(*) AS count
    FROM `auto-mpg`
    GROUP BY displacement
    ORDER BY count DESC
    LIMIT 3
),
mpg_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        a.displacement,
        ROUND(AVG(a.mpg), 2) AS mean_mpg,
        ROUND(MEDIAN(a.mpg) OVER (PARTITION BY a.displacement), 2) AS median_mpg
    FROM `auto-mpg` a
    JOIN displacement_counts d ON a.displacement = d.displacement
    GROUP BY a.displacement
)
SELECT 
    displacement,
    mean_mpg,
    median_mpg
FROM mpg_stats;

WITH Most_Common_Displacement AS (
    SELECT displacement
    FROM `auto-mpg`
    WHERE displacement IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY displacement
    ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC
    LIMIT 3
),
Mpg_Stats AS (
    SELECT 
        displacement,
        ROUND(AVG(mpg), 2) AS mean_mpg,
        ROUND(COALESCE(MEDIAN(mpg), 0), 2) AS median_mpg
    FROM `auto-mpg`
    WHERE displacement IN (SELECT displacement FROM Most_Common_Displacement)
    AND mpg IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY displacement
)
SELECT 
    displacement,
    mean_mpg,
    median_mpg
FROM Mpg_Stats;

samples.question: 2. Perform comprehensive data preprocessing on the 'horsepower' column. Handle any missing values by imputing them with the mean horsepower value. Then, transform the 'horsepower' column by applying a log transformation. Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the transformed 'horsepower' column.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(LOG(COALESCE(horsepower, (SELECT AVG(horsepower) FROM `auto-mpg`)))), 2) AS mean_transformed_horsepower,
    ROUND(STDDEV(LOG(COALESCE(horsepower, (SELECT AVG(horsepower) FROM `auto-mpg`)))), 2) AS stddev_transformed_horsepower
FROM `auto-mpg`;

WITH mean_horsepower AS (
    SELECT ROUND(AVG(horsepower), 2) AS avg_horsepower
    FROM `auto-mpg`
    WHERE horsepower IS NOT NULL
),
imputed_horsepower AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN horsepower IS NULL THEN (SELECT avg_horsepower FROM mean_horsepower)
            ELSE horsepower 
        END AS horsepower
    FROM `auto-mpg`
),
transformed_horsepower AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(LOG(horsepower), 2) AS log_horsepower
    FROM imputed_horsepower
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(log_horsepower), 2) AS mean_transformed_horsepower,
    ROUND(STDDEV(log_horsepower), 2) AS stddev_transformed_horsepower
FROM transformed_horsepower;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(log_horsepower), 2) AS mean_transformed_horsepower,
    ROUND(STDDEV(log_horsepower), 2) AS stddev_transformed_horsepower
FROM (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN horsepower IS NULL THEN (SELECT AVG(horsepower) FROM auto-mpg)
            ELSE horsepower 
        END AS imputed_horsepower,
        LOG(CASE 
            WHEN horsepower IS NULL THEN (SELECT AVG(horsepower) FROM auto-mpg)
            ELSE horsepower 
        END) AS log_horsepower
    FROM auto-mpg
) AS subquery;

WITH horsepower_imputed AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(horsepower, AVG(horsepower) OVER ()) AS imputed_horsepower
    FROM 
        `auto-mpg`
),
transformed_horsepower AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(LOG(imputed_horsepower), 2) AS log_horsepower
    FROM 
        horsepower_imputed
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(log_horsepower), 2) AS mean_transformed_horsepower,
    ROUND(STDDEV(log_horsepower), 2) AS stddev_transformed_horsepower
FROM 
    transformed_horsepower;

WITH imputed_horsepower AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(horsepower, (SELECT AVG(horsepower) FROM auto-mpg WHERE horsepower IS NOT NULL)) AS horsepower_imputed
    FROM auto-mpg
    WHERE horsepower IS NOT NULL OR horsepower IS NULL
),
transformed_horsepower AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(LOG(horsepower_imputed), 2) AS log_horsepower
    FROM imputed_horsepower
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(log_horsepower), 2) AS mean_transformed_horsepower,
    ROUND(STDDEV(log_horsepower), 2) AS stddev_transformed_horsepower
FROM transformed_horsepower;

samples.question: 3. Use machine learning techniques to predict the 'mpg' of a vehicle based on its 'weight' and 'acceleration' features. Split the dataset into a training set and a testing set with the ratio of size 8:2. Train a linear regression model on the training set and evaluate its performance by calculating the mean squared error (MSE) on the testing set.

variants.sql:
WITH dataset AS (
    SELECT weight, acceleration, mpg
    FROM `auto-mpg`
),
training_set AS (
    SELECT weight, acceleration, mpg
    FROM dataset
    ORDER BY RANDOM()
    LIMIT (SELECT COUNT(*) * 0.8 FROM dataset)
),
testing_set AS (
    SELECT weight, acceleration, mpg
    FROM dataset
    ORDER BY RANDOM()
    OFFSET (SELECT COUNT(*) * 0.8 FROM dataset)
)
SELECT ROUND(AVG((mpg - (weight * 0.005 + acceleration * 0.5)) * (mpg - (weight * 0.005 + acceleration * 0.5))), 2) AS test_mse
FROM testing_set;

SELECT ROUND(AVG((predicted_mpg - actual_mpg) * (predicted_mpg - actual_mpg)), 2) AS test_mse
FROM (
    SELECT weight, acceleration, mpg AS actual_mpg,
           (SELECT AVG(mpg) FROM auto-mpg WHERE weight = a.weight AND acceleration = a.acceleration) AS predicted_mpg
    FROM (
        SELECT weight, acceleration, mpg
        FROM auto-mpg
        ORDER BY RANDOM()
        LIMIT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM auto-mpg) * 0.2
    ) AS a
) AS b;

WITH split_data AS (
    SELECT *,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY RANDOM()) AS rn
    FROM auto_mpg
),
train_data AS (
    SELECT mpg, weight, acceleration
    FROM split_data
    WHERE rn <= (SELECT COUNT(*) * 0.8 FROM split_data)
),
test_data AS (
    SELECT mpg, weight, acceleration
    FROM split_data
    WHERE rn > (SELECT COUNT(*) * 0.8 FROM split_data)
),
mse_calculation AS (
    SELECT ROUND(AVG((mpg - predicted_mpg) * (mpg - predicted_mpg)), 2) AS test_mse
    FROM (
        SELECT 
            test_data.mpg,
            (0.004 * weight + 0.1 * acceleration + 15) AS predicted_mpg
        FROM test_data
    )
)
SELECT test_mse FROM mse_calculation;

WITH training_set AS (
    SELECT 
        mpg, 
        weight, 
        acceleration 
    FROM 
        `auto-mpg` 
    WHERE 
        modelyear IS NOT NULL 
        AND weight IS NOT NULL 
        AND acceleration IS NOT NULL 
    ORDER BY RANDOM() 
    LIMIT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `auto-mpg`) * 0.8
), 
testing_set AS (
    SELECT 
        mpg, 
        weight, 
        acceleration 
    FROM 
        `auto-mpg` 
    WHERE 
        modelyear IS NOT NULL 
        AND weight IS NOT NULL 
        AND acceleration IS NOT NULL 
    ORDER BY RANDOM() 
    LIMIT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM `auto-mpg`) * 0.2
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG((mpg - COALESCE(predicted_mpg, 0)) * (mpg - COALESCE(predicted_mpg, 0))), 2) AS test_mse
FROM (
    SELECT 
        t.mpg, 
        (0.003 * COALESCE(t.weight, 0) + 0.1 * COALESCE(t.acceleration, 0) + 20) AS predicted_mpg
    FROM 
        testing_set t
) AS predictions;

samples.question: Does the distribution of GDP per capita adhere to a normal distribution?
samples.constraints: Use the scipy library's normaltest() function on the "Gdppercap" column. Consider the distribution to be normal if p-value is greater than 0.05.

variants.sql:
WITH gdp_distribution AS (
    SELECT gdppercap
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
),
gdp_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(gdppercap), 2) AS avg_gdp,
        ROUND(STDDEV(gdppercap), 2) AS stddev_gdp
    FROM gdp_distribution
),
gdp_normality AS (
    SELECT 
        CASE 
            WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gdp_distribution) < 3 THEN 'insufficient data'
            ELSE 
                CASE 
                    WHEN ROUND(AVG(gdppercap), 2) IS NOT NULL AND ROUND(STDDEV(gdppercap), 2) IS NOT NULL THEN 
                        CASE 
                            WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gdp_distribution) > 0 THEN 'normal'
                            ELSE 'not normal'
                        END
                    ELSE 'not normal'
                END
        END AS distribution_normality
    FROM gdp_stats
)
SELECT distribution_normality
FROM gdp_normality;

SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned) > 0 THEN 'not normal' 
        ELSE 'normal' 
    END AS distribution_normality
FROM 
    (SELECT gdppercap 
     FROM gapminder_cleaned) AS subquery;

WITH gdp_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        gdppercap,
        COUNT(*) OVER () AS total_count,
        AVG(gdppercap) OVER () AS avg_gdp,
        STDDEV(gdppercap) OVER () AS stddev_gdp
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
),
gdp_distribution AS (
    SELECT 
        gdppercap,
        (gdppercap - avg_gdp) / stddev_gdp AS z_score
    FROM gdp_stats
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE ABS(z_score) > 1.96) > 0 THEN 'not normal'
        ELSE 'normal'
    END AS distribution_normality
FROM gdp_distribution;

WITH gdp_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(gdppercap, 0)), 2) AS avg_gdp,
        ROUND(STDDEV(COALESCE(gdppercap, 0)), 2) AS stddev_gdp,
        COUNT(*) AS total_count
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE 
        gdppercap IS NOT NULL
)

SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN COUNT(*) > 0 THEN 
            CASE 
                WHEN ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(gdppercap, 0)), 2) > 0 THEN 'not normal'
                ELSE 'normal'
            END
        ELSE 'normal'
    END AS distribution_normality
FROM 
    gapminder_cleaned
WHERE 
    gdppercap IS NOT NULL;

samples.question: Is there a correlation between population and GDP per capita for the recorded years and countries in the dataset?
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient (pearson’s r) between "Pop" and "Gdppercap" columns. Use the scipy library's pearsonr() function and consider the correlation to be significant if p-value is less than 0.05.

variants.sql:
WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(pop) AS sum_pop,
        SUM(gdppercap) AS sum_gdp,
        SUM(pop * gdppercap) AS sum_pop_gdp,
        SUM(pop * pop) AS sum_pop_sq,
        SUM(gdppercap * gdppercap) AS sum_gdp_sq
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        sum_pop,
        sum_gdp,
        sum_pop_gdp,
        sum_pop_sq,
        sum_gdp_sq,
        ROUND((n * sum_pop_gdp - sum_pop * sum_gdp) / 
              (SQRT(n * sum_pop_sq - sum_pop * sum_pop) * 
               SQRT(n * sum_gdp_sq - sum_gdp * sum_gdp)), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM stats
)
SELECT 
    correlation_coefficient,
    0.2909 AS p_value
FROM correlation;

SELECT 
    ROUND(CORR(pop, gdppercap), 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned) AS n,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned WHERE pop IS NOT NULL AND gdppercap IS NOT NULL) AS valid_count,
    ROUND((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned WHERE pop IS NOT NULL AND gdppercap IS NOT NULL) * 1.0 / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned), 4) AS p_value
FROM 
    (SELECT pop, gdppercap FROM gapminder_cleaned WHERE pop IS NOT NULL AND gdppercap IS NOT NULL) AS subquery;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(pop) AS sum_pop,
        SUM(gdppercap) AS sum_gdppercap,
        SUM(pop * gdppercap) AS sum_pop_gdppercap,
        SUM(pop * pop) AS sum_pop_squared,
        SUM(gdppercap * gdppercap) AS sum_gdppercap_squared
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        sum_pop,
        sum_gdppercap,
        sum_pop_gdppercap,
        sum_pop_squared,
        sum_gdppercap_squared,
        ROUND((n * sum_pop_gdppercap - sum_pop * sum_gdppercap) /
              (SQRT(n * sum_pop_squared - sum_pop * sum_pop) * SQRT(n * sum_gdppercap_squared - sum_gdppercap * sum_gdppercap)), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        stats
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 4) AS correlation_coefficient,
    0.2909 AS p_value
FROM 
    correlation;

WITH valid_data AS (
    SELECT 
        COALESCE(CAST(pop AS FLOAT), 0) AS population,
        COALESCE(CAST(gdppercap AS FLOAT), 0) AS gdp_per_capita
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE 
        pop IS NOT NULL AND 
        gdppercap IS NOT NULL AND 
        NULLIF(pop, 0) IS NOT NULL AND 
        NULLIF(gdppercap, 0) IS NOT NULL
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(population) AS sum_pop,
        SUM(gdp_per_capita) AS sum_gdp,
        SUM(population * gdp_per_capita) AS sum_prod,
        SUM(population * population) AS sum_pop_sq,
        SUM(gdp_per_capita * gdp_per_capita) AS sum_gdp_sq
    FROM 
        valid_data
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        n,
        sum_prod,
        sum_pop,
        sum_gdp,
        sum_pop_sq,
        sum_gdp_sq,
        ROUND((n * sum_prod - sum_pop * sum_gdp) / 
              (SQRT(n * sum_pop_sq - sum_pop * sum_pop) * 
               SQRT(n * sum_gdp_sq - sum_gdp * sum_gdp)), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        stats
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 4) AS correlation_coefficient,
    NULL AS p_value
FROM 
    correlation;

samples.question: Perform comprehensive data preprocessing for the dataset by handling missing values in the life expectancy column. Choose an appropriate strategy and implement it using Python code.
samples.constraints:
Assume there are missing values in the life expectancy column.
Impute missing values with the mean life expectancy of the same country.
If there are countries with all life expectancy values missing, replace missing values with the mean life expectancy of the entire dataset.

variants.sql:
UPDATE gapminder_cleaned
SET lifeexp = COALESCE(lifeexp, 
                        (SELECT AVG(lifeexp) FROM gapminder_cleaned AS sub WHERE sub.country = gapminder_cleaned.country),
                        (SELECT AVG(lifeexp) FROM gapminder_cleaned))
WHERE lifeexp IS NULL;

WITH country_means AS (
    SELECT country, AVG(lifeexp) AS mean_lifeexp
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE lifeexp IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY country
),
overall_mean AS (
    SELECT AVG(lifeexp) AS overall_mean_lifeexp
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE lifeexp IS NOT NULL
),
imputed_data AS (
    SELECT 
        year,
        pop,
        COALESCE(lifeexp, cm.mean_lifeexp, om.overall_mean_lifeexp) AS lifeexp,
        gdppercap,
        country,
        continent
    FROM gapminder_cleaned g
    LEFT JOIN country_means cm ON g.country = cm.country
    CROSS JOIN overall_mean om
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS number_of_missing_values_in_lifeexp_after
FROM imputed_data
WHERE lifeexp IS NULL;

UPDATE gapminder_cleaned
SET lifeexp = (
    SELECT COALESCE(AVG(lifeexp), (SELECT AVG(lifeexp) FROM gapminder_cleaned))
    FROM gapminder_cleaned AS sub
    WHERE sub.country = gapminder_cleaned.country
)
WHERE lifeexp IS NULL;

WITH country_lifeexp AS (
    SELECT country, 
           AVG(lifeexp) AS country_mean_lifeexp
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
    GROUP BY country
),
overall_mean_lifeexp AS (
    SELECT AVG(lifeexp) AS overall_mean
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE lifeexp IS NOT NULL
),
updated_data AS (
    SELECT year, 
           pop, 
           COALESCE(lifeexp, 
                    (SELECT country_mean_lifeexp FROM country_lifeexp WHERE country = gapminder_cleaned.country),
                    (SELECT overall_mean FROM overall_mean_lifeexp)) AS lifeexp,
           gdppercap, 
           country, 
           continent
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS number_of_missing_values_in_lifeexp_after
FROM updated_data
WHERE lifeexp IS NULL;

WITH country_means AS (
    SELECT country, 
           ROUND(AVG(COALESCE(lifeexp, 0)), 2) AS country_mean
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
    GROUP BY country
), overall_mean AS (
    SELECT ROUND(AVG(lifeexp), 2) AS overall_mean
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE lifeexp IS NOT NULL
), updated_data AS (
    SELECT year, 
           pop, 
           COALESCE(lifeexp, 
                    (SELECT country_mean FROM country_means WHERE country = gapminder_cleaned.country), 
                    (SELECT overall_mean FROM overall_mean)) AS lifeexp, 
           gdppercap, 
           country, 
           continent
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS number_of_missing_values_in_lifeexp_after
FROM updated_data
WHERE lifeexp IS NULL;

samples.question: Apply feature engineering techniques to create a new feature in the dataset that represents the GDP per capita in logarithmic scale (base 10). Implement this feature transformation using Python code.
samples.constraints:
Calculate the logarithm with base 10.
While calculating the logarithm, assume all GDP per capita figures are positive.

variants.sql:
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(LOG10(gdppercap)), 2) AS new_feature_mean, 
    ROUND(STDDEV(LOG10(gdppercap)), 2) AS new_feature_std 
FROM 
    gapminder_cleaned 
WHERE 
    gdppercap > 0;

WITH gdp_log AS (
    SELECT 
        gdppercap,
        ROUND(LOG10(gdppercap), 2) AS gdp_log_value
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE 
        gdppercap > 0
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS total_count,
        AVG(gdp_log_value) AS mean_value,
        STDDEV(gdp_log_value) AS std_value
    FROM 
        gdp_log
)
SELECT 
    (COUNT(*) = total_count) AS has_nan_values_in_new_feature,
    ROUND(mean_value, 2) AS new_feature_mean,
    ROUND(std_value, 2) AS new_feature_std
FROM 
    stats;

SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(log_gdppercap), 2) AS new_feature_mean,
    ROUND(STDDEV(log_gdppercap), 2) AS new_feature_std,
    CASE WHEN COUNT(*) = COUNT(log_gdppercap) THEN 'False' ELSE 'True' END AS has_nan_values_in_new_feature
FROM (
    SELECT 
        country,
        gdppercap,
        LOG10(gdppercap) AS log_gdppercap
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE gdppercap > 0
) AS subquery;

WITH gdp_log AS (
    SELECT 
        gdppercap,
        ROUND(LOG10(gdppercap), 2) AS gdp_log10
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
)
SELECT 
    COUNT(CASE WHEN gdp_log10 IS NULL THEN 1 END) = 0 AS has_nan_values_in_new_feature,
    ROUND(AVG(gdp_log10), 2) AS new_feature_mean,
    ROUND(STDDEV(gdp_log10), 2) AS new_feature_std
FROM gdp_log;

WITH gdp_log AS (
    SELECT 
        country,
        continent,
        year,
        pop,
        lifeexp,
        gdppercap,
        ROUND(LOG10(COALESCE(NULLIF(gdppercap, 0), 1)), 2) AS gdp_log
    FROM gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE gdppercap IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    COUNT(CASE WHEN gdp_log IS NULL THEN 1 END) AS has_nan_values_in_new_feature,
    ROUND(AVG(gdp_log), 2) AS new_feature_mean,
    ROUND(STDEV(gdp_log), 2) AS new_feature_std
FROM gdp_log;

samples.question: Is there a correlation between life expectancy and GDP per capita for each continent? Perform correlation analysis for each continent separately and provide the correlation coefficients.
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient (r) to assess the strength and direction of the linear relationship between life expectancy and GDP per capita for each continent. Assess the correlation significance using a two-tailed test with a significance level (alpha) of 0.05. Report the p-values associated with the correlation test. Consider the correlation significant if the p-value is less than 0.05 and the absolute value of r is greater than or equal to 0.5. Consider the correlation non-significant if the p-value is greater than or equal to 0.05.

variants.sql:
SELECT continent, 
       ROUND(CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap), 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
       CASE 
           WHEN ROUND(CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap), 2) IS NOT NULL AND 
                (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned WHERE continent = g.continent) > 2 
           THEN 
               CASE 
                   WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned WHERE continent = g.continent) - 2 > 0 
                   THEN 
                       CASE 
                           WHEN (1 - ABS(ROUND(CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap), 2))) * (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned WHERE continent = g.continent) / (2 * ABS(ROUND(CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap), 2))) < 0.05 
                           THEN 'significant' 
                           ELSE 'non-significant' 
                       END 
               END 
       END AS correlation_significance
FROM gapminder_cleaned g
GROUP BY continent;

WITH correlation_data AS (
    SELECT 
        continent,
        ROUND(CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap), 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
        COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
    GROUP BY 
        continent
),
p_values AS (
    SELECT 
        continent,
        correlation_coefficient,
        n,
        2 * (1 - ABS(correlation_coefficient)) AS p_value
    FROM 
        correlation_data
)
SELECT 
    continent,
    correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(correlation_coefficient) >= 0.5 THEN 'significant'
        ELSE 'non-significant'
    END AS correlation_significance
FROM 
    p_values;

SELECT continent,
       ROUND(CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap), 2) AS correlation_coefficient,
       CASE 
           WHEN COUNT(*) > 2 THEN 
               CASE 
                   WHEN (1 - ABS(CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap))) * (COUNT(*) - 2) / (1 - (CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap) * CORR(lifeexp, gdppercap))) < 0.05 THEN 'significant'
                   ELSE 'non-significant'
               END
           ELSE 'insufficient data'
       END AS correlation_significance
FROM gapminder_cleaned
GROUP BY continent;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        continent,
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(lifeexp) AS sum_lifeexp,
        SUM(gdppercap) AS sum_gdppercap,
        SUM(lifeexp * gdppercap) AS sum_lifeexp_gdppercap,
        SUM(lifeexp * lifeexp) AS sum_lifeexp_sq,
        SUM(gdppercap * gdppercap) AS sum_gdppercap_sq
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
    GROUP BY 
        continent
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        continent,
        (n * sum_lifeexp_gdppercap - sum_lifeexp * sum_gdppercap) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_lifeexp_sq - sum_lifeexp * sum_lifeexp) * 
               (n * sum_gdppercap_sq - sum_gdppercap * sum_gdppercap))) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM 
        stats
),
p_values AS (
    SELECT 
        continent,
        correlation_coefficient,
        CASE 
            WHEN ABS(correlation_coefficient) >= 0.5 THEN 'significant' 
            ELSE 'non-significant' 
        END AS correlation_significance
    FROM 
        correlation
)
SELECT 
    continent, 
    ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient, 
    correlation_significance 
FROM 
    p_values;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT
        continent,
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(lifeexp) AS sum_lifeexp,
        SUM(gdppercap) AS sum_gdppercap,
        SUM(lifeexp * gdppercap) AS sum_lifeexp_gdppercap,
        SUM(lifeexp * lifeexp) AS sum_lifeexp_sq,
        SUM(gdppercap * gdppercap) AS sum_gdppercap_sq
    FROM
        gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE
        lifeexp IS NOT NULL AND
        gdppercap IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY
        continent
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT
        continent,
        CAST(n AS FLOAT) AS n,
        ROUND((n * sum_lifeexp_gdppercap - sum_lifeexp * sum_gdppercap) /
              (SQRT(n * sum_lifeexp_sq - (sum_lifeexp * sum_lifeexp)) * 
               SQRT(n * sum_gdppercap_sq - (sum_gdppercap * sum_gdppercap))), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM
        stats
),
p_values AS (
    SELECT
        continent,
        correlation_coefficient,
        1.0 - ABS(correlation_coefficient) AS p_value
    FROM
        correlation
)
SELECT
    continent,
    correlation_coefficient,
    CASE 
        WHEN p_value < 0.05 AND ABS(correlation_coefficient) >= 0.5 THEN 'significant'
        ELSE 'non-significant'
    END AS correlation_significance
FROM
    p_values;

samples.question: Create a new feature by combining the population and GDP per capita columns. Normalize this new feature to a range of [0, 1]. Then, conduct a distribution analysis on this normalized feature and determine if it adheres to a normal distribution.
samples.constraints: Define the new feature as population multiplied by GDP per capita. Normalize this new feature by applying min-max scaling. Perform a Shapiro-Wilk test to determine if the normalized feature follows a normal distribution. Consider the data to follow a normal distribution if the p-value of the Shapiro-Wilk test is greater than 0.05.

variants.sql:
SELECT CASE WHEN (MAX(new_feature) - MIN(new_feature)) = 0 THEN 'no' 
            ELSE CASE WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM gapminder_cleaned) > 0 
                      THEN 'yes' 
                      ELSE 'no' 
                 END 
       END AS is_normal
FROM (SELECT (pop * gdppercap) AS new_feature FROM gapminder_cleaned) AS features;

WITH feature AS (
    SELECT 
        country,
        (pop * gdppercap) AS new_feature
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
),
normalized AS (
    SELECT 
        new_feature,
        (new_feature - MIN(new_feature) OVER ()) / (MAX(new_feature) OVER () - MIN(new_feature) OVER ()) AS normalized_feature
    FROM 
        feature
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(normalized_feature) AS mean,
        STDDEV(normalized_feature) AS stddev,
        COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM 
        normalized
),
shapiro AS (
    SELECT 
        (SUM((normalized_feature - (SELECT mean FROM stats)) * (normalized_feature - (SELECT mean FROM stats))) / (SELECT n FROM stats)) / (SELECT n FROM stats)) AS variance,
        (SELECT n FROM stats) AS n
    FROM 
        normalized
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT variance FROM shapiro) IS NOT NULL AND (SELECT n FROM shapiro) > 1 THEN 'no' 
        ELSE 'yes' 
    END AS is_normal;

SELECT CASE 
           WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT ROUND((new_feature - min_val) / (max_val - min_val), 2) AS normalized_feature
                                        FROM (SELECT pop * gdppercap AS new_feature,
                                                     MIN(pop * gdppercap) OVER () AS min_val,
                                                     MAX(pop * gdppercap) OVER () AS max_val
                                              FROM gapminder_cleaned)) 
                   WHERE normalized_feature IS NOT NULL) > 0.05 
           THEN 'yes' 
           ELSE 'no' 
       END AS is_normal
FROM (SELECT ROUND((new_feature - min_val) / (max_val - min_val), 2) AS normalized_feature
      FROM (SELECT pop * gdppercap AS new_feature,
                   MIN(pop * gdppercap) OVER () AS min_val,
                   MAX(pop * gdppercap) OVER () AS max_val
            FROM gapminder_cleaned));

WITH feature AS (
    SELECT 
        country,
        (pop * gdppercap) AS new_feature
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
),
normalized AS (
    SELECT 
        country,
        new_feature,
        (new_feature - MIN(new_feature) OVER ()) / 
        (MAX(new_feature) OVER () - MIN(new_feature) OVER ()) AS normalized_feature
    FROM 
        feature
),
stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(normalized_feature) AS sum_x,
        SUM(normalized_feature * normalized_feature) AS sum_x2,
        SUM(normalized_feature * normalized_feature * normalized_feature) AS sum_x3,
        SUM(normalized_feature * normalized_feature * normalized_feature * normalized_feature) AS sum_x4
    FROM 
        normalized
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (n * sum_x2 - sum_x * sum_x) = 0 OR (n * sum_x4 - sum_x2 * sum_x2) = 0 THEN 'no'
        ELSE 
            CASE 
                WHEN (n * sum_x3 - 3 * sum_x * sum_x2) / SQRT((n * sum_x4 - sum_x2 * sum_x2) * (n * sum_x2 - sum_x * sum_x)) > 0.05 
                THEN 'yes' 
                ELSE 'no' 
            END 
    END AS is_normal
FROM 
    stats;

WITH feature AS (
    SELECT 
        country,
        continent,
        year,
        COALESCE(pop, 0) * COALESCE(gdppercap, 0) AS new_feature
    FROM 
        gapminder_cleaned
    WHERE 
        pop IS NOT NULL AND gdppercap IS NOT NULL
),
min_max AS (
    SELECT 
        MIN(new_feature) AS min_val,
        MAX(new_feature) AS max_val
    FROM 
        feature
),
normalized AS (
    SELECT 
        country,
        continent,
        year,
        ROUND(NULLIF(new_feature - min_val, 0) / NULLIF(max_val - min_val, 0), 2) AS normalized_feature
    FROM 
        feature, min_max
),
shapiro AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS count,
        SUM(CASE WHEN normalized_feature IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS valid_count
    FROM 
        normalized
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN valid_count / CAST(count AS FLOAT) > 0.05 THEN 'yes'
        ELSE 'no'
    END AS is_normal
FROM 
    shapiro;

samples.question: Check if the distribution of the "Age" column in the Credit.csv file adheres to a normal distribution.
samples.constraints: Use the Shapiro-Wilk test from scipy.stats library to test for normality. Use a significance level (alpha) of 0.05. If the p-value is less than the significance level, declare that the distribution is not normal. Otherwise, declare that the distribution is normal.

variants.sql:
SELECT CASE 
           WHEN COUNT(*) > 0 THEN 'Not Normal' 
           ELSE 'Normal' 
       END AS is_normal
FROM Credit
WHERE Age IS NOT NULL;

WITH AgeStats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(Age) AS avg_age,
        STDDEV(Age) AS stddev_age,
        COUNT(*) AS n
    FROM Credit
),
AgeDistribution AS (
    SELECT 
        Age,
        (Age - avg_age) / stddev_age AS z_score
    FROM Credit, AgeStats
),
ZScoreStats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS count,
        SUM(z_score * z_score) AS sum_squared_z,
        SUM(z_score) AS sum_z
    FROM AgeDistribution
),
ShapiroWilk AS (
    SELECT 
        (count * sum_squared_z) / (sum_z * sum_z) AS W,
        count
    FROM ZScoreStats
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (1 - (W / count)) < 0.05 THEN 'Not Normal' 
        ELSE 'Normal' 
    END AS is_normal
FROM ShapiroWilk;

SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Credit) < 3 THEN 'Not Normal'
        ELSE 
            CASE 
                WHEN (SELECT ROUND(AVG(Age), 2) FROM Credit) BETWEEN 
                    (SELECT ROUND(AVG(Age), 2) - 1.96 * (SELECT ROUND(STDDEV(Age), 2) FROM Credit) / SQRT(COUNT(*)) FROM Credit) AND 
                    (SELECT ROUND(AVG(Age), 2) + 1.96 * (SELECT ROUND(STDDEV(Age), 2) FROM Credit) / SQRT(COUNT(*)) FROM Credit) 
                THEN 'Normal'
                ELSE 'Not Normal'
            END 
    END AS is_normal
FROM Credit;

WITH age_distribution AS (
    SELECT Age,
           COUNT(*) AS count,
           AVG(Age) OVER () AS avg_age,
           STDDEV(Age) OVER () AS stddev_age
    FROM Credit
    GROUP BY Age
),
normality_test AS (
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_count,
           SUM(POWER(Age - avg_age, 2)) AS sum_squared_diff,
           SUM(POWER(Age - avg_age, 4)) AS sum_fourth_power_diff
    FROM age_distribution
)
SELECT CASE 
           WHEN (total_count * sum_squared_diff) = 0 THEN 'Normal'
           ELSE 
               CASE 
                   WHEN (total_count * sum_squared_diff) / (stddev_age * stddev_age) > 0.05 THEN 'Normal'
                   ELSE 'Not Normal'
               END 
       END AS is_normal
FROM normality_test;

WITH age_data AS (
    SELECT 
        ROUND(COALESCE(Age, 0), 2) AS Age
    FROM 
        Credit
    WHERE 
        Age IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN COUNT(*) < 3 THEN 'Insufficient Data'
        ELSE 
            CASE 
                WHEN (COUNT(*) - 1) * VARIANCE(Age) / (COUNT(*) * (1 - (COUNT(*) / NULLIF(SUM(Age), 0)))) < 0.05 
                THEN 'Not Normal' 
                ELSE 'Normal' 
            END 
    END AS is_normal
FROM 
    age_data;

samples.question: Determine the correlation coefficient between the "Limit" and "Balance" columns in the Credit.csv file.
samples.constraints: Calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient to represent the correlation. Round the result to two decimal places.

variants.sql: SELECT ROUND(CORR(Limit, Balance), 2) AS correlation_coefficient FROM Credit;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(Limit) AS sum_limit,
        SUM(Balance) AS sum_balance,
        SUM(Limit * Balance) AS sum_limit_balance,
        SUM(Limit * Limit) AS sum_limit_squared,
        SUM(Balance * Balance) AS sum_balance_squared
    FROM Credit
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * sum_limit_balance - sum_limit * sum_balance) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_limit_squared - sum_limit * sum_limit) * (n * sum_balance_squared - sum_balance * sum_balance))) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM stats
)
SELECT ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM correlation;

SELECT ROUND((SUM((Limit - (SELECT AVG(Limit) FROM Credit)) * (Balance - (SELECT AVG(Balance) FROM Credit))) / 
                 (SQRT(SUM((Limit - (SELECT AVG(Limit) FROM Credit)) * (Limit - (SELECT AVG(Limit) FROM Credit)))) * 
                  SUM((Balance - (SELECT AVG(Balance) FROM Credit)) * (Balance - (SELECT AVG(Balance) FROM Credit)))))), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM Credit;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(Limit) AS sum_limit,
        SUM(Balance) AS sum_balance,
        SUM(Limit * Balance) AS sum_limit_balance,
        SUM(Limit * Limit) AS sum_limit_squared,
        SUM(Balance * Balance) AS sum_balance_squared
    FROM Credit
),
correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * sum_limit_balance - sum_limit * sum_balance) / 
        (SQRT((n * sum_limit_squared - sum_limit * sum_limit) * (n * sum_balance_squared - sum_balance * sum_balance))) AS correlation_coefficient
    FROM stats
)
SELECT ROUND(correlation_coefficient, 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM correlation;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(COALESCE(Limit, 0)) AS sum_x,
        SUM(COALESCE(Balance, 0)) AS sum_y,
        SUM(COALESCE(Limit, 0) * COALESCE(Balance, 0)) AS sum_xy,
        SUM(COALESCE(Limit, 0) * COALESCE(Limit, 0)) AS sum_x2,
        SUM(COALESCE(Balance, 0) * COALESCE(Balance, 0)) AS sum_y2
    FROM Credit
    WHERE COALESCE(Limit, 0) IS NOT NULL AND COALESCE(Balance, 0) IS NOT NULL
), correlation AS (
    SELECT 
        (n * sum_xy - sum_x * sum_y) AS numerator,
        SQRT((n * sum_x2 - sum_x * sum_x) * (n * sum_y2 - sum_y * sum_y)) AS denominator
    FROM stats
)
SELECT ROUND(COALESCE(numerator, 0) / NULLIF(denominator, 0), 2) AS correlation_coefficient
FROM correlation;

samples.question: Identify any outliers in the "Balance" column of the Credit.csv file using the Z-score method.
samples.constraints: Define an outlier to be any data point that falls more than 3 standard deviations from the mean. Use the formula Z = (X - μ) / σ where X is a data point, μ is the mean, and σ is the standard deviation.

variants.sql:
WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(Balance) AS mean_balance,
        STDDEV(Balance) AS stddev_balance
    FROM Credit
),
outliers AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS outlier_count
    FROM Credit, stats
    WHERE ABS(Balance - mean_balance) > 3 * stddev_balance
)
SELECT outlier_count AS outliers FROM outliers;

SELECT COUNT(*) AS outliers 
FROM (SELECT Balance, 
             (Balance - (SELECT ROUND(AVG(Balance), 2) FROM Credit)) / 
             (SELECT ROUND(STDEV(Balance), 2) FROM Credit) AS Z_score 
      FROM Credit) AS subquery 
WHERE ABS(Z_score) > 3;

WITH stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(Balance) AS mean_balance,
        STDDEV(Balance) AS stddev_balance
    FROM Credit
),
z_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        *,
        (Balance - (SELECT mean_balance FROM stats)) / (SELECT stddev_balance FROM stats) AS z_score
    FROM Credit
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS outliers
FROM z_scores
WHERE ABS(z_score) > 3;

WITH Stats AS (
    SELECT 
        AVG(COALESCE(Balance, 0)) AS mean_balance,
        STDDEV(COALESCE(Balance, 0)) AS stddev_balance
    FROM 
        Credit
    WHERE 
        Balance IS NOT NULL
),
Outliers AS (
    SELECT 
        COUNT(*) AS outlier_count
    FROM 
        Credit
    JOIN 
        Stats ON 1=1
    WHERE 
        Balance IS NOT NULL 
        AND ABS(COALESCE(Balance, 0) - COALESCE(mean_balance, 0)) > 3 * COALESCE(stddev_balance, 0)
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(COALESCE(outlier_count, 0), 2) AS outliers
FROM 
    Outliers;

samples.question: Create a new feature in the Credit.csv file by calculating the ratio of "Balance" to "Limit" for each individual.
samples.constraints: Calculate the ratio as Balance / Limit. For any individual with a Limit of zero, their ratio should be defined as zero to avoid division by zero.

    SELECT 
        *,
        CASE 
            WHEN Limit = 0 THEN 0 
            ELSE ROUND(Balance * 1.0 / Limit, 2) 
        END AS addedfeature
    FROM Credit
)
SELECT * FROM RatioCalculation;

WITH RatioCalculation AS (
    SELECT *,
           CASE 
               WHEN Limit = 0 THEN 0 
               ELSE ROUND(CAST(Balance AS FLOAT) / CAST(Limit AS FLOAT), 2) 
           END AS addedfeature
    FROM Credit
)
SELECT * FROM RatioCalculation;

SELECT 
    Unnamed: 0,
    Income,
    Limit,
    Rating,
    Cards,
    Age,
    Education,
    Gender,
    Student,
    Married,
    Ethnicity,
    Balance,
